proposals.title: Bidirectional Consistency Training for Robust Conditional Sequence Generation
proposals.motivation: Standard conditional sequence models (e.g., for translation, summarization) are trained to maximize P(target|source), which can lead to brittle, one-sided mappings and poor handling of ambiguous or noisy inputs. This unidirectional objective ignores the inherent duality and consistency required for robust, human-like understanding and generation, a core theme in Cho's work on feedback and system-level interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: Jointly training a model to predict both P(target|source) and P(source|target) will enforce a latent consistency that improves robustness, sample efficiency, and output coherence compared to standard unidirectional training.
proposals.method: We propose a consistency training framework where a single transformer-based model is trained with a combined objective: the standard negative log-likelihood for the forward direction and a reconstruction loss for the inverse direction. Inspired by denoising autoencoders and noise-invariant training methods, we will augment this with a 'subread' corruption process on either source or target sequences, tasking the model with recovering the original clean pair. This bidirectional denoising objective will be optimized alongside the primary conditional task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training paradigm for conditional generation that yields more robust and internally consistent models. It will offer new insights into learning invertible representations for sequences, with potential applications in machine translation, dialogue systems, and data augmentation where bidirectional fidelity is valuable.
proposals.novelty: While inverse models exist for analysis, the novelty lies in the tight, joint training of bidirectional conditional distributions within a single model using a consistency-driven, denoising objective, moving beyond simple product-of-experts or separate model ensembles common in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Enhancing Encoder-Decoder Architectures with Gated Feedback and High-Order Recurrent Units for Long-Range Sequence Modeling
proposals.motivation: The encoder-decoder framework with simple RNNs (e.g., tanh units) has been foundational for sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation. However, these models struggle with learning long-range dependencies due to vanishing gradients and limited state dynamics. Improving the core recurrent unit is crucial for tasks requiring the integration of distant context, such as document-level translation or coherent long-form text generation, where current models exhibit performance degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the simple tanh RNN in encoder-decoder models with more sophisticated recurrent units that explicitly model multi-step dependencies and incorporate gated feedback mechanisms will significantly improve the learning of long-range dependencies and overall sequence modeling performance.
proposals.method: We will systematically integrate and evaluate advanced RNN units into a standard encoder-decoder framework. Specifically, we will test Gated Feedback RNNs (GF-RNNs), which allow error signals to flow through multiple stacked layers via recurrent gating, and High-Order RNNs (HORNNs), which capture interactions between multiple previous hidden states. We will benchmark these against LSTM/GRU-based baselines on tasks like character-level language modeling (Penn Treebank) and long-context machine translation (e.g., WMT English-German with document-level context), measuring performance, gradient flow, and effective context length.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical analysis of under-explored RNN units within the modern encoder-decoder paradigm, moving beyond the LSTM/GRU dichotomy. It will deliver concrete architectural recommendations and open-source implementations for improving long-range dependency modeling in sequence-to-sequence learning, potentially bridging a gap towards more efficient alternatives to full attention mechanisms for very long sequences.
proposals.novelty: While LSTM/GRU variants dominate, a systematic pre-2026 exploration of GF-RNNs and HORNNs in encoder-decoder architectures for NLP is lacking. This proposal uniquely combines the themes of feedback (via GF-RNNs) and sophisticated sequence modeling (via HORNNs) within Cho's research trajectory, offering a novel synthesis rather than incremental unit optimization.

proposals.title: Decomposing Learned Phrase Representations: A Qualitative Analysis of Syntactic and Semantic Feature Emergence
proposals.motivation: While large language models demonstrate impressive performance, the interpretability of their internal representations, particularly for multi-word phrases, remains a black box. Understanding what syntactic structures and semantic relations these phrase vectors encode is crucial for building more robust, controllable, and trustworthy models. This analysis, grounded in pre-2026 interpretability research, seeks to bridge the gap between model performance and human-understandable feature decomposition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that phrase representations in transformer-based language models decompose into linearly separable subspaces corresponding to discrete syntactic roles (e.g., subject, modifier) and semantic properties (e.g., sentiment, domain), and that this decomposition becomes more consistent and human-aligned with scale and targeted training feedback.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of probing classifiers, representation similarity analysis (RSA), and controlled intervention techniques on models like T5 or BART. Building on Cho's work on feedback and consistency, we will design a novel 'subread' probing framework where models are trained to predict fine-grained, human-annotated linguistic features of phrases extracted from their intermediate representations. We will further use consistency training objectives to encourage alignment between the model's internal feature decomposition and external linguistic knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed qualitative taxonomy of the linguistic features captured in phrase-level representations, moving beyond single-word analysis. It will yield a diagnostic toolkit for evaluating representation quality and offer insights for improving model efficiency and generalization through more structured latent spaces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted, multi-granularity analysis of phrase representations—a level between tokens and sentences often overlooked—and the integration of consistency-based training from Cho's recent methods to steer and analyze feature emergence, rather than just passively probing a static model.

proposals.title: Sequence-Level Policy Optimization with Natural Language Feedback for Non-Differentiable Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Current sequence generation models, particularly in machine translation, are trained via maximum likelihood estimation, which suffers from exposure bias and a mismatch with non-differentiable end-task metrics like BLEU or ROUGE. Directly optimizing for these metrics is crucial for improving generation quality, but the discrete nature of text makes gradient-based optimization challenging. This problem is central to bridging the gap between model training and human-centric evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning framework, using policy gradient methods like REINFORCE or PPO to optimize sequence-level rewards, can be effectively guided by system-level natural language feedback to improve the stability and sample efficiency of training for discrete text generation tasks.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., Transformer) as a stochastic policy. The action space is the vocabulary, and a complete generated sequence is a trajectory. The reward will be a composite function combining a task-specific metric (e.g., BLEU) and a learned reward model trained on system-level natural language feedback (inspired by Cho's prior work) to provide denser, more informative learning signals. We will employ the REINFORCE algorithm with a baseline (e.g., a value network) for variance reduction and investigate proximal policy optimization (PPO) for improved training stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust framework for direct sequence-level optimization of text generation models, moving beyond teacher forcing. It will demonstrate how integrating learned feedback models can make RL training for NLP more practical and sample-efficient. The resulting methodology will be applicable to a range of generation tasks where evaluation metrics are non-differentiable.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of a learned model of system-level natural language feedback as a dense reward shaping component within a policy gradient framework, moving beyond sparse, delayed metric-based rewards. This directly connects Cho's themes of feedback, sequence-level learning, and human-centric evaluation in a novel RL-based training paradigm.

proposals.title: Learning a Structured Latent Space for Natural Language with a Feedback-Guided Hierarchical VAE
proposals.motivation: Standard sequence-to-sequence models for language lack a structured, continuous latent representation that captures semantic and syntactic hierarchies, limiting controllable generation and interpretability. Pre-2026, VAEs applied to text often produce poor reconstructions or collapse to a prior, failing to leverage the rich, multi-level feedback inherent in human language understanding and generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical variational autoencoder (VAE) for sequences, trained with a novel consistency objective derived from system-level natural language feedback, can learn a disentangled latent space where subspaces correspond to distinct linguistic levels (e.g., syntax, semantics, discourse).
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical VAE where the encoder uses a transformer to map a phrase to a sequence of latent variables (z1,...,zL), each modeling a different linguistic level. The decoder will be autoregressive, conditioned on this hierarchy. Training will integrate a reconstruction loss, the standard VAE KL penalty, and a novel consistency loss: we will generate subreads (e.g., paraphrases, ablated sequences) from manipulated latents and enforce that the encoder maps these variants to consistently manipulated latent codes, using feedback functions inspired by Cho's prior work to define meaningful transformations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new generative model for language that provides an interpretable, structured latent representation, enabling fine-grained control over generated text. It will also advance training methods for latent variable models on discrete sequences by demonstrating how consistency constraints and feedback can stabilize training and improve latent space organization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a hierarchical latent structure for language with a training objective that enforces consistency across linguistically meaningful transformations, moving beyond standard VAE training which focuses solely on reconstruction and prior matching.

proposals.title: Character-Level Sequence-to-Sequence Learning with Subword Consistency and Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Modern NLP models operate primarily on subword tokens, which introduce a fixed, language-specific vocabulary that limits robustness to misspellings, code-switching, and novel morphological forms. A return to character-level modeling, informed by recent advances in sequence-to-sequence architectures and consistency training, could yield more flexible and data-efficient models. This aligns with the researcher's interest in fundamental sequence modeling and system-level feedback, moving beyond the word-level bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: An encoder-decoder model trained directly on character sequences, using consistency objectives across sub-reads of the input and fine-tuned with natural language feedback, can achieve or surpass the performance of subword-based models on tasks like machine translation and grammatical error correction, while demonstrating superior robustness to noise and morphological complexity.
proposals.method: We will adapt a standard Transformer encoder-decoder to process raw character sequences. To address the long-sequence challenge, we will employ a training method inspired by "subreads," where the model is trained to produce consistent outputs given different, randomly sampled subsequences (character n-grams) of the input. This consistency objective will be combined with standard cross-entropy loss. Subsequently, the model will be fine-tuned using a human-in-the-loop framework, where natural language feedback (e.g., "the translation is too formal") is provided to iteratively refine model outputs, directly applying the researcher's prior work on system-level feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a modern, rigorous evaluation of character-level seq2seq models, demonstrating their viability as an alternative to subword tokenization. It will yield new techniques for training on very long sequences via consistency and for integrating high-level human feedback at the character level. The resulting models are expected to be more robust and adaptable, particularly for low-resource or morphologically rich languages.
proposals.novelty: While character-level RNNs exist, applying the full Transformer encoder-decoder at the character level with a consistency training objective across sub-reads is novel. Furthermore, integrating high-level natural language feedback to guide character-generation decisions represents an unexplored intersection of granular sequence modeling and human-in-the-loop learning.

proposals.title: Learning Discrete Text Bottlenecks: A Feedback-Driven Approach to Neural Compression and Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models (LLMs) are powerful but data-hungry, requiring immense storage and bandwidth for text corpora. Traditional lossless compression is limited, while existing neural text compression lacks robust, trainable methods for high-fidelity reconstruction. This work addresses the need for intelligent, lossy compression that prioritizes semantic preservation and downstream task utility, inspired by information bottlenecks in representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: An encoder-decoder model trained with a consistency objective on discrete, subword-level latent codes can function as an effective lossy compressor, where the compressed representation maintains high utility for both accurate reconstruction and semantic-level tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder will map a sequence of subword tokens to a shorter sequence of discrete latent codes via a learned quantization bottleneck, inspired by vector quantization methods. A key innovation is the training regimen: we will employ a consistency loss, ensuring the decoder can reconstruct the original sequence from these codes, coupled with a feedback-driven objective using a frozen LLM to assess semantic preservation. This mimics 'system-level natural language feedback' to guide compression towards retaining human-relevant information.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a novel neural text compression model that provides tunable compression ratios with quantifiable semantic fidelity. It contributes a framework for integrating task-level feedback directly into compression training. The resulting 'dual-use' codes could serve as efficient data representations for storage, transmission, or as inputs to other models.
proposals.novelty: While autoencoders exist, the integration of a discrete bottleneck with a consistency objective and LLM-based semantic feedback for text compression is novel. This moves beyond simple reconstruction error, explicitly optimizing the compressed code for high-level, human-centric information content.

proposals.title: Character-Level Consistency Training for Efficient and Robust Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Subword tokenization, while standard, introduces a fixed, brittle vocabulary that struggles with rare words, misspellings, and domain shifts. A pure character-level model could offer superior flexibility and robustness, but historically suffers from slow training and poor long-range coherence compared to subword models, limiting its practical adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying consistency training objectives—inspired by recent successes in image denoising and sequence alignment—directly to a character-level sequence model, we can bridge the performance gap with subword models, achieving competitive perplexity and downstream task accuracy while retaining the inherent advantages of character-level inputs.
proposals.method: We will develop a character-level encoder-decoder Transformer, treating the input as a sequence of Unicode bytes or characters. The core innovation is a training procedure that enforces consistency: we will create noisy versions of input sequences (via character swaps, deletions, or insertions) and train the model to map both the original and noised sequences to the same latent representation or output distribution. This draws from denoising autoencoder principles and the "subreads" concept for robust sequence understanding. We will compare against standard BPE-based baselines on language modeling and system-level feedback tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a viable path to high-performance, vocabulary-free language models, reducing engineering overhead for new domains or languages. It will provide new insights into how consistency objectives can stabilize training for fine-grained sequence generation, contributing to the fields of efficient NLP and robust sequence-to-sequence learning.
proposals.novelty: While character-level models exist, combining them with modern consistency training techniques from other domains (e.g., denoising U-Nets for images, alignment methods for proteins) to solve their historical instability is a novel synthesis. It inverts the standard pipeline by forcing the model to learn robust compositions from the start, rather than relying on a pre-defined subword vocabulary.

proposals.title: Learning to Prune: Dynamic Vocabulary Subset Selection via Consistency Training and Human-in-the-Loop Feedback
proposals.motivation: Autoregressive decoding in large neural machine translation models is computationally expensive, as the softmax operation scales with vocabulary size. Current static or frequency-based candidate subset selection methods are crude and can degrade translation quality by pruning contextually critical words. A more intelligent, dynamic pruning mechanism is needed to maintain quality while enabling faster, more efficient decoding.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, context-aware selector network can be trained via consistency objectives and human-level feedback to dynamically prune the full vocabulary to a small, high-likelihood subset at each decoding step, significantly reducing computation without sacrificing translation fidelity.
proposals.method: We will train a small, auxiliary 'selector' module (e.g., a lightweight transformer or LSTM) that takes the decoder's current hidden state and source context as input to predict a sparse mask over the full vocabulary. This module will be trained using a multi-stage approach: first, via consistency distillation from a teacher model's full-distribution attention; second, using a reinforcement learning objective shaped by system-level natural language feedback (e.g., quality metrics like BLEURT) to align pruning decisions with human judgments of translation quality. The selector will be integrated into the decoder's softmax layer for dynamic subset selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel decoding algorithm that reduces inference latency and memory footprint for large-scale NMT. It will advance the integration of learned, adaptive sparsity into sequence generation and provide a framework for using human-simulated feedback to optimize sub-component decisions in a generative pipeline.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static pruning or fixed shortlists, our method learns to make dynamic, context-dependent pruning decisions. The integration of consistency training from a teacher model with a feedback-driven RL objective for vocabulary selection is a novel synthesis of techniques from Cho's recent work on consistency, subreads, and system-level feedback.

proposals.title: Large-Vocabulary Importance Sampling for Consistent and Controllable Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive language models suffer from exposure bias and degenerate, repetitive outputs during generation. Techniques like nucleus sampling or beam search offer limited control and can produce inconsistent text. A principled, sampling-based method that directly optimizes for sequence-level consistency and integrates human feedback signals is needed to improve generation quality in tasks like summarization and dialogue.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the large-vocabulary importance sampling technique, inspired by methods for training consistency models in continuous domains, to discrete text generation will yield sequences with higher global coherence and better alignment with human preferences than standard decoding strategies.
proposals.method: We will adapt the training and sampling framework of consistency models (Song et al., 2023) to the discrete sequence space of language. A denoising network (e.g., a Transformer) will be trained to map noisy sequences (from a forward diffusion process over tokens or embeddings) back to clean data. For generation, we will perform importance sampling over the large vocabulary at each step, guided by the consistency model's trajectory, and integrate a learned reward model (trained on human feedback) to bias sampling towards high-quality, task-specific outputs (e.g., factual summaries).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel non-autoregressive decoding strategy that directly optimizes for sequence-level properties. It will demonstrate improved performance on text summarization and open-ended generation benchmarks, measured by metrics like coherence, diversity, and human evaluation scores. The method offers a unified framework for incorporating different forms of feedback into the generation process.
proposals.novelty: While consistency models and diffusion exist for images, their application to discrete text with large-vocabulary importance sampling is nascent. Combining this with a system-level feedback mechanism for controllable generation is a novel synthesis of Cho's research themes.

proposals.title: Efficient Neural Machine Translation via Negative Sampling for Target Vocabulary Approximation
proposals.motivation: The computational bottleneck in Neural Machine Translation (NMT) is the softmax normalization over the entire target vocabulary, which scales poorly for large vocabularies and long sequences. While negative sampling is a proven, efficient method for training word embeddings in models like word2vec, its direct application to the structured prediction task of NMT remains underexplored. This project addresses the core efficiency challenge in NMT training by adapting a classic, scalable technique to a modern sequence-to-sequence context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the negative sampling objective from word2vec to approximate the NMT softmax will significantly reduce training time and memory footprint while preserving, or even improving, translation quality through a more efficient and noise-contrastive learning signal.
proposals.method: We will integrate negative sampling into the standard encoder-decoder NMT architecture (e.g., Transformer). Instead of computing a full softmax over the target vocabulary for every decoder step, we will define a noise-contrastive estimation (NCE) loss. For each target word (positive sample), we will sample a small number of negative words from a dynamically adjusted noise distribution (e.g., based on unigram frequency). The model will be trained to distinguish the true next word from these negative samples. We will experiment with strategies for integrating this objective, including a hybrid loss that combines standard cross-entropy for high-frequency words with NCE for the long tail.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for training large-vocabulary NMT models more efficiently, reducing both time and hardware requirements. It will also contribute a theoretical and empirical analysis of how noise-contrastive estimation interacts with autoregressive sequence generation, bridging the gap between static embedding models and dynamic conditional language models.
proposals.novelty: While negative sampling is well-known in embedding learning, its application to the conditional generative process of NMT is non-trivial and novel. The key innovation lies in formulating the sequential, context-dependent prediction of NMT as a series of contrastive learning problems, moving beyond its use in static representation learning.

proposals.title: Dynamic Vocabulary Biasing via Neural Cache for Efficient and Context-Aware Decoding
proposals.motivation: Autoregressive decoding in large language models is computationally expensive, as the softmax operation scales with vocabulary size. While methods like adaptive softmax and vocabulary pruning exist, they are often static or rely on simple heuristics, failing to dynamically adapt to the immediate context of the generation process. A more efficient, context-sensitive method for candidate vocabulary selection is needed to reduce inference cost without sacrificing output quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a lightweight, continuously updated neural cache model—inspired by k-nearest-neighbor language models—into the decoding loop can dynamically and effectively bias the candidate vocabulary subset, leading to faster generation and improved contextual coherence compared to static pruning methods.
proposals.method: We propose augmenting a standard Transformer decoder with a differentiable cache module that stores recent hidden states and corresponding token labels. During each decoding step, the cache computes a similarity distribution between the current hidden state and cached keys, which is then interpolated with the primary model's logit distribution. This interpolated distribution will be used to select a dynamic, top-k candidate vocabulary subset for the full softmax computation. We will adapt training techniques from consistency-based learning to ensure the primary model and the cache module are jointly optimized for this selective decoding objective, preventing distributional mismatch.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, efficient decoding framework that reduces inference latency and memory footprint. It will also offer insights into integrating fast, local memory mechanisms with global parametric models, advancing the field of efficient sequence generation. The method will be evaluated on standard language modeling and machine translation benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static or frequency-based vocabulary pruning, our method uses a learned, dynamic cache to inform vocabulary selection in real-time based on the generative context. Furthermore, it integrates this cache not as a retrieval-augmented post-hoc step, but as a core, trainable component of the decoding architecture, aligning with Cho's research themes of feedback and sequence-level learning.

proposals.title: DUAL: Efficient Source-Side Vocabulary Scaling via Differentiable Unembedding Alignment Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern neural machine translation (NMT) and multilingual models face a fundamental asymmetry: target-side vocabularies are efficiently managed via subword segmentation (e.g., BPE), but source-side vocabularies for languages with large character sets (e.g., Chinese, Japanese) or rich morphology remain a bottleneck. Directly scaling the source embedding matrix leads to prohibitive memory and computational costs, hindering the inclusion of many languages in a single model. This work addresses the inverse of the common subword problem: how to efficiently encode from a massive, discrete source symbol set.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a source vocabulary can be represented by a compact, trainable codebook, where the embeddings for a vast set of surface forms are generated on-demand by a lightweight, parameter-efficient function, maintaining model performance while drastically reducing static parameters.
proposals.method: We propose DUAL, a method where a small base embedding matrix covers a frequent subset. For any out-of-base source symbol, its embedding is generated by a learned, deterministic function (e.g., a small MLP or hypernetwork) that operates on a fixed feature representation of the symbol (e.g., character n-grams or phonetic hashes). This function is trained with a consistency loss, ensuring that generated embeddings for rare symbols behave similarly to learned embeddings for frequent ones within the model's forward pass. We will integrate this within a standard Transformer encoder and train on massively multilingual data (e.g., OPUS) to evaluate translation quality and embedding stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for scaling NMT and multilingual language models to source languages with very large vocabularies without linear parameter growth. It will yield insights into the representational capacity of generated versus stored embeddings. The resulting open-source code will lower the barrier to inclusive, large-scale multilingual modeling.
proposals.novelty: While subword methods compress the target/generation space, we explicitly tackle compression of the source/encoding space via a trainable, on-demand embedding generator. This inverts the standard vocabulary problem and connects to Cho's themes of efficient representation and consistency-based learning, but applies it to the largely underexplored challenge of source-side scalability.

proposals.title: Memory-Augmented Neural Machine Translation via Feedback-Driven Subread Consistency
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation (NMT) struggles with long-range dependencies, such as maintaining pronoun consistency or thematic coherence across paragraphs. Pre-2026, models like the Transformer rely on fixed-length attention windows, which can fail to capture distant contextual cues. External memory mechanisms, inspired by work in Question Answering (e.g., Memory Networks), offer a promising but underexplored path for NMT to explicitly store and retrieve long-spanning discourse information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a translation model equipped with a differentiable, content-addressable external memory, trained with a consistency objective on 'subreads' (coherent text segments), will significantly improve translation quality on documents with long-range dependencies by effectively propagating context across sentence boundaries.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based NMT model by integrating a recurrent external memory module, similar to a Key-Value Memory Network. The model will be trained using a multi-task objective combining standard cross-entropy loss with a novel consistency loss. This loss will enforce that 'subreads'—overlapping segments of the source and target—produce consistent memory representations and translations. Training will leverage back-translation for data augmentation and employ scheduled sampling to bridge training and inference use of the memory.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel NMT architecture capable of handling document-level context, moving beyond sentence-level translation. It will provide a new training methodology using subread consistency for stabilizing memory-augmented models. The resulting model is expected to show measurable gains on established document-level translation benchmarks (e.g., WMT, IWSLT) and in maintaining discourse phenomena like lexical cohesion.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic adaptation of QA-style memory networks to NMT, combined with a training regime that uses subread-level consistency feedback—a concept from the researcher's recent work—to guide the memory's learning process, which is a non-obvious fusion of these pre-2026 themes and methods.

proposals.title: Learning to Compress and Expand: A Dual-Task Framework for Controllable Text Simplification via Natural Language Feedback
proposals.motivation: Sentence compression is a core task for improving readability and information density, but pre-2026 models often produce rigid, ungrammatical, or meaning-altering outputs. Furthermore, the process is typically unidirectional, lacking a mechanism to verify or refine the compressed output. This limits practical applications where preserving semantic intent is critical, such as in assistive technologies or content adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing sentence compression as a dual task—jointly learning to compress and then reconstruct the original sentence from the compression—regularized by system-level natural language feedback, will yield more fluent, grammatical, and semantically faithful compressions than standard encoder-decoder models trained solely on compression.
proposals.method: We will employ a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model trained with a dual objective: a primary compression loss and a secondary reconstruction loss (expanding the compression back towards the original). Inspired by consistency training and Cho's work on feedback, we will incorporate a feedback loop where a separate critic model, trained on human preferences, provides natural language directives (e.g., "too short," "missing verb") on the compression. These directives will be encoded as subreads and fed back into the decoder for iterative refinement, creating a training signal that enforces semantic consistency and grammaticality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for text compression that explicitly enforces semantic fidelity through a cycle-consistency loss and interactive feedback. It will advance methods for integrating natural language feedback into sequence-to-sequence tasks at the system level. The resulting model will provide more controllable and reliable sentence simplifications for downstream NLP applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit dual-task formulation for monolingual summarization, combined with an iterative refinement mechanism driven by learned natural language feedback, moving beyond static sequence-to-sequence training towards a more interactive and self-correcting generation process.

proposals.title: Adaptive State Expansion: Scaling Transformer Capacity Dynamically with Input Sequence Length
proposals.motivation: Current Transformer models use a fixed parameter set regardless of input length, leading to potential under-parameterization for long, complex sequences and inefficient over-parameterization for short ones. This rigidity limits efficiency and expressivity in tasks like long-document understanding, code generation, and structured prediction, where the required model capacity is intrinsically linked to the input's structural complexity and length. An adaptive model could allocate computational resources more intelligently, improving performance and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically scaling the latent state dimension or the number of effective parameters in a Transformer's feed-forward networks in proportion to input sequence length will lead to more sample-efficient learning and superior generalization on variable-length sequence tasks, compared to fixed-capacity baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a Transformer variant where the hidden dimension of feed-forward layers (or a supplementary state vector) expands linearly or sub-linearly with sequence length. This will be implemented via a learnable, length-dependent projection that maps a base hidden state to an expanded representation, inspired by adaptive computation time and conditional computation. We will integrate consistency training objectives to ensure stable learning despite variable parameterization, and employ subread-style segmental processing to manage extremely long sequences. Evaluation will span language modeling, remote homology detection (aligning with Cho's recent work), and algorithmic reasoning tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for building length-adaptive neural architectures, providing a concrete method to scale model capacity with problem difficulty. It will yield empirical evidence on the benefits of dynamic parameterization for sequence modeling. The resulting models will offer a more flexible trade-off between computational cost and accuracy for variable-length inputs.
proposals.novelty: While most efficient Transformers focus on reducing compute for long sequences, we propose to *increase* model capacity adaptively, tying parameterization directly to input length. This inverts the standard efficiency goal to prioritize expressivity where needed, combining ideas from conditional computation and structured state-space models in a novel, length-conditioned way.

proposals.title: A Neural Operator Framework for the Systematic Analysis and Generation of Recurrent Network Architectures
proposals.motivation: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful framework for learning mappings between function spaces, showing strong performance in scientific computing tasks like PDE solving. However, their analysis has largely been confined to feedforward architectures like DeepONets and Fourier Neural Operators. Concurrently, recurrent neural networks (RNNs), especially modern variants like LSTMs and GRUs, remain central to sequence modeling but are often designed heuristically. A principled, operator-theoretic analysis of RNNs is lacking, limiting our ability to understand their function-space properties and systematically improve them.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural operator framework can be extended to formally characterize a broad class of recurrent architectures as parameterized operators on sequence spaces, and that this characterization will enable the principled generation of novel, more efficient recurrent networks with provable stability and approximation guarantees.
proposals.method: We will first formalize popular RNN cells (e.g., GRU, LSTM) as discrete-time neural operators acting on a hidden state function space. We will then analyze their properties—such as stability, Lipschitz continuity, and approximation capacity—using operator-theoretic tools. Building on this analysis and inspired by recent methods in consistency training and sub-read modeling, we will propose a novel architecture generation procedure. This procedure will use a learned consistency loss to enforce desired operator properties (e.g., contractivity) and will employ sub-read mechanisms to efficiently handle long-range dependencies, leading to new, generated RNN cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unifying theoretical lens for understanding RNNs, moving beyond empirical design. It will yield a practical methodology for generating novel recurrent architectures with tailored stability and efficiency properties. The resulting models will be validated on challenging sequence modeling benchmarks, including those requiring long-context understanding and system-level natural language feedback tasks.
proposals.novelty: While neural operators have been applied to static function mappings, applying this framework to the dynamic, iterative structure of RNNs is novel. Furthermore, using the framework not just for analysis but for the systematic generation of new architectures, guided by operator-theoretic constraints, represents a significant departure from existing heuristic or purely search-based architecture design methods.

proposals.title: Learning to Attend to the Relevantly Irrelevant: Auxiliary Temporal Guidance for Latent Feature Recovery in Transformers
proposals.motivation: Standard transformer attention mechanisms, while powerful, can converge to focus on a narrow set of obvious, short-range dependencies, potentially missing subtle, long-range, or counter-intuitive cues that are nonetheless predictive. This limits model robustness and generalization, particularly in tasks requiring inference over extended contexts or where critical signals are distributed and non-salient. A method to guide attention towards such 'relevantly irrelevant' features without disrupting core language modeling could unlock more sophisticated sequence understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight auxiliary network, trained with a consistency objective to predict future or held-out context, can identify temporal segments whose latent features, when emphasized, improve downstream prediction, and that softly guiding a primary transformer's attention with this signal will improve its ability to model complex temporal dependencies.
proposals.method: We augment a transformer language model with a parallel Temporal Relevance Critic (TRC), a small transformer or LSTM that processes the same token sequence. The TRC is trained with an auxiliary loss (e.g., contrastive or regression) to predict a future segment or a masked global context, creating a saliency map over input tokens. A gating mechanism (e.g., a learned sigmoid gate per attention head) uses this map to modulate the base model's attention weights, softly amplifying focus on TRC-identified segments. The entire system is trained end-to-end, balancing the primary language modeling loss with the auxiliary TRC objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel training paradigm for learning to recover latent-temporal features, moving beyond supervised attention or hard constraints. It will provide a concrete architecture and method for integrating auxiliary, task-agnostic guidance into transformer attention, potentially improving performance on tasks requiring long-range reasoning. The concept of 'relevant irrelevance' offers a new lens for analyzing what models learn to attend to.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on supervised attention or attention distillation, the critic is not trained on human annotations but on a self-supervised, temporal consistency objective, creating a naturally adversarial signal to discover non-obvious dependencies. The soft gating mechanism preserves the base model's flexibility, avoiding the pitfalls of forcing attention onto noise or predefined patterns.

proposals.title: Surprise-Gated Temporal Attention: Improving Language Model Robustness via Predictive Coding
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive language models attend to context based on learned static patterns, which can lead to brittle performance on rare events or surprising narrative turns. Drawing from predictive processing theories in neuroscience, which posit that learning is driven by minimizing surprise, offers a principled alternative. This project aims to integrate this biologically-inspired principle into sequence modeling to improve robustness and sample-efficient learning from anomalies.
proposals.hypothesis: Biasing a transformer's attention mechanism towards tokens that violate a model's own lightweight, top-down predictions will lead to more robust representations and improved handling of rare or counterfactual linguistic events compared to standard attention.
proposals.method: We will augment a standard transformer decoder with a parallel, lightweight predictive network (e.g., a small LSTM or linearized self-attention layer) that runs a few steps ahead to generate a distribution over upcoming context. The primary model's attention scores will then be modulated by a gating function (e.g., a multiplicative bias) proportional to the predictive surprise (KL divergence) between the prediction and the actual input. This creates a feedback loop where attention is dynamically focused on surprising subsequences, which will be trained end-to-end with a consistency loss encouraging the main model to explain the surprise.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, neurally-plausible attention mechanism that explicitly optimizes for surprise minimization. It will provide a new framework for incorporating feedback and predictive processing into large language models, with potential applications in few-shot learning and robustness to distribution shifts. The project will also deliver empirical results on tasks requiring adaptation to rare syntactic structures or narrative anomalies.
proposals.novelty: While predictive coding is studied in neuroscience and some machine learning areas, its direct application to gate temporal attention in transformers via a lightweight, parallel predictive model is novel. This inverts the standard attention paradigm by making salience a function of dynamic prediction error rather than static learned affinity.

proposals.title: Counterfactual Sequence Modeling via Temporal Convolutional Attention
proposals.motivation: Standard self-attention in transformers, while powerful, can be myopic to long-range, multi-scale periodic dependencies in sequences, such as discourse structure in language or seasonal patterns in time-series. This limits model robustness and interpretability, especially for tasks requiring reasoning about alternative sequence histories or latent temporal phases. A mechanism to actively restructure attention based on learned temporal patterns could unlock new capabilities in sequence modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating learnable temporal convolutional filters into the attention mechanism will enable models to discover and attend to predictive multi-scale periodic patterns, thereby improving performance on counterfactual and causal reasoning tasks over sequences where standard attention fails.
proposals.method: We will design a Temporal Convolutional Attention (TCA) block that operates in parallel with standard self-attention. A set of 1D convolutional kernels will be learned over the temporal dimension of the input sequence embeddings, producing multi-scale feature maps highlighting phased patterns. These feature maps will be projected and used to compute a dynamic bias or gating signal that re-weights the standard attention distribution. The entire architecture will be trained end-to-end, with the convolutional kernels optimized via backpropagation to extract signals that improve a downstream objective, such as sequence generation under intervention or next-step prediction with missing context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, hybrid attention mechanism that fuses convolutional inductive bias with adaptive attention. It will provide a concrete method for improving model sensitivity to latent temporal structure, with empirical validation on tasks like system-level natural language feedback generation and time-series forecasting under interventions. The resulting models will offer better interpretability through the learned convolutional filters.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in simply adding convolutions to transformers, but in using learned temporal convolutional features to dynamically and fundamentally reshape the attention distribution itself, creating an active, co-adaptive perception system for sequences. This moves beyond static or local convolutional additions seen in prior work like ConvTransformers.

proposals.title: Inverting Temporal Attention: Improving Language Model Robustness via Feedback-Driven Focus on Neglected Context
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive language models rely heavily on a recency bias in their attention mechanisms, potentially overlooking critical information in earlier or seemingly irrelevant temporal segments of a context. This can lead to failures in tasks requiring long-range reasoning, consistency, or the integration of distant cues, limiting model robustness and interpretability. Addressing this bias aligns with the broader goal of developing models that more comprehensively understand and utilize sequence information.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly training a language model to attend to temporal segments it initially deems irrelevant—effectively inverting its default attention focus—we can improve its performance on tasks requiring the synthesis of information distributed across long contexts and enhance its robustness to distracting or complex narrative structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage training framework. First, we will analyze attention patterns in a base transformer (e.g., GPT-2/3 architecture) to identify 'neglected' tokens based on consistently low attention scores. Second, we will employ a consistency training objective, inspired by denoising and feedback methods, where the model is trained to reconstruct or answer queries using primarily these neglected segments, penalizing reliance on the initially high-attention tokens. This could involve a custom attention masking strategy during training or an auxiliary loss that maximizes attention entropy across the temporal sequence, guided by human or synthetic feedback on desired reasoning paths.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training methodology for manipulating temporal attention biases in autoregressive models. It will provide new insights into the relationship between attention patterns and model capability, potentially leading to more robust and context-aware language models. The resulting models and analysis tools will be valuable for applications in long-form question answering, coherent story generation, and factual consistency checking.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed or regularized attention, the explicit inversion of a model's innate temporal attention focus via a targeted, feedback-driven training objective is a novel intervention. This moves beyond simple analysis or uniform smoothing towards actively reshaping the model's information-seeking behavior based on its own identified weaknesses.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Attention for Multi-Scale Video Understanding with Natural Language Feedback
proposals.motivation: Video understanding requires integrating information across multiple temporal scales, from short actions to long-term narrative structure, a challenge analogous to modeling documents with words, sentences, and paragraphs. Pre-2026 video models often rely on fixed-scale temporal pooling or simple recurrent networks, which struggle to explicitly capture this hierarchical structure. This limits their ability to provide coherent, high-level descriptions or answer complex queries about video content.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical attention network, inspired by successful document modeling architectures, can more effectively model video semantics by learning to attend to relevant segments at frame, shot, and scene levels, and that this representation can be refined through iterative natural language feedback from a human user.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN) architecture, where the first level uses a transformer encoder to attend over frames within a short segment (e.g., 1 second), and the second level uses another transformer to attend over the encoded segment representations. This builds a multi-scale video encoding. We will integrate this into a training loop inspired by 'System-Level Natural Language Feedback': a human (or simulated user) provides textual feedback on a model's initial video caption or QA response, and the model uses a consistency objective to update its hierarchical attention weights to align its internal representations with the corrective feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel neural architecture for hierarchical video representation learning, explicitly bridging techniques from NLP and vision. It will also demonstrate a concrete framework for incorporating system-level natural language feedback into the training of complex, structured vision models, advancing the theme of human-in-the-loop learning.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical attention exists in NLP, its application to video with an integrated feedback loop is novel. The key innovation is the combination of a structured, multi-scale video encoder with a feedback-driven training mechanism to dynamically adjust attention based on high-level human guidance.

proposals.title: Long-Form Video Description via Hierarchical Consistency Training and Iterative Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Current video captioning models excel at short clips but struggle with long-form videos, producing fragmented or incoherent multi-sentence paragraphs. This limits practical applications in automated content summarization, accessibility, and video indexing. A method that generates coherent, contextually consistent narratives for long videos remains an open challenge in video-language understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical training strategy, enforcing consistency across different temporal granularities (frames, shots, scenes), combined with iterative refinement using system-level natural language feedback, will enable the generation of coherent and detailed long-form video paragraphs.
proposals.method: We will extend a sequence-to-sequence architecture (e.g., a video-adapted Transformer) with a multi-level encoder processing subreads (short video segments) and a hierarchical decoder. The core training will use a novel consistency loss, inspired by Cho's recent work, that aligns narrative flow across these levels. We will then employ an iterative fine-tuning loop where a critic model, trained on human preferences, provides system-level natural language feedback to refine paragraph coherence and factual accuracy, similar to the feedback methods in Cho's recent research.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel model and training framework for long-form video paragraph generation, a new benchmark dataset with long videos and paragraph-level annotations, and a methodology for integrating hierarchical consistency constraints with iterative feedback-driven refinement in video-language tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of hierarchical consistency training (from subreads to full narrative) with a system-level feedback loop for sequential video description, a combination not explored in pre-2026 video captioning literature, which primarily focuses on single-sentence generation or lacks structured multi-level coherence mechanisms.

proposals.title: AdapterFusion for Sequence-to-Sequence Learning: Efficient Multi-Task and Transfer Learning for Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Adapter-based tuning is a parameter-efficient method for adapting large pre-trained models to new tasks, but its application to sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) generation remains underexplored. Pre-2026, AdapterFusion has shown promise in classification tasks for combining knowledge from multiple adapters, yet its potential for complex generation tasks like translation and summarization is unknown. This gap limits the efficient reuse and composition of specialized skills in large language models for text generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the AdapterFusion framework to seq2seq architectures will enable effective, parameter-efficient multi-task learning and transfer for generation, outperforming single adapters and full fine-tuning in cross-task generalization, especially in low-resource scenarios.
proposals.method: We will extend the AdapterFusion method to Transformer-based seq2seq models (e.g., BART, T5). Adapters will be trained independently on diverse seq2seq tasks (e.g., translation, summarization, paraphrasing). A novel fusion layer will then be trained to learn a dynamic, context-dependent composition of these frozen adapters' outputs during generation. We will experiment with different fusion mechanisms (e.g., attention-based) and integration points (e.g., within encoder, decoder, or cross-attention blocks) to optimize for generation quality and consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive framework for parameter-efficient, composable multi-task learning for text generation. It will deliver a method to combine specialized generation skills without catastrophic forgetting, enabling more flexible and sample-efficient adaptation of large pre-trained models. The resulting models and code will serve as a foundation for research into modular, reusable NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: While AdapterFusion exists for encoder-only models, its principled adaptation and evaluation for the more complex, autoregressive dynamics of seq2seq generation is novel. This work bridges the gap between parameter-efficient adaptation and the compositional needs of multi-faceted text generation tasks.

proposals.title: The Expressive Power of Sequence Models: A Piecewise-Linear Analysis of Deep Convolutional and Recurrent Architectures
proposals.motivation: Understanding the expressive capacity of deep networks is a fundamental problem in deep learning theory. Prior to 2026, the number of linear regions in deep feedforward ReLU networks has been studied as a measure of their ability to approximate complex functions, but a rigorous comparative analysis for convolutional (CNNs) and recurrent architectures (RNNs/LSTMs/GRUs) is lacking. This gap is critical, as these architectures are the backbone of sequence modeling in language and biological data, areas central to the researcher's themes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep convolutional networks, due to weight sharing and local connectivity, induce a fundamentally different scaling of linear regions with depth and width compared to recurrent networks, which process sequences iteratively. This difference quantifiably impacts their theoretical approximation power for sequential data.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of linear region counting to CNNs and RNNs with ReLU activations. For CNNs, we will analyze the effect of convolution, pooling, and depth on the partitioning of the input space. For RNNs, we will treat the unfolded network as a deep feedforward network with parameter tying and analyze region growth across time steps. We will derive theoretical bounds and complement them with empirical measurements using algorithms for region enumeration on low-dimensional synthetic tasks and higher-dimensional image/sequence patches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical characterization and direct comparison of the piecewise-linear complexity of major sequential architectures. It will yield concrete bounds that inform architecture selection based on desired model capacity. The findings will bridge theoretical expressivity with practical model design for sequence and language tasks.
proposals.novelty: While linear regions have been studied for fully-connected networks, applying this analysis to convolutional and recurrent structures with parameter sharing is novel. The direct comparative framework between these two foundational architecture families for sequence processing does not exist in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Amortized and Sparse Dynamic Refinement of Encoder Representations for Efficient Sequence-to-Sequence Learning
proposals.motivation: Standard encoder-decoder models compute a static, one-pass encoder representation, which can be insufficient for complex reasoning or disambiguation tasks requiring deeper analysis of the source. Dynamic refinement of encoder states during decoding, as in the Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) for encoders, is prohibitively expensive for large-scale models. This work addresses the critical need for a mechanism that provides dynamic, context-aware encoder representations without the quadratic or recurrent computational overhead of naive approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decoder can learn to sparsely trigger lightweight, amortized refinements of cached encoder states, and that these targeted updates will significantly improve model performance on tasks requiring nuanced source understanding, while keeping computational cost close to that of a static encoder.
proposals.method: We will augment a standard Transformer encoder-decoder. A small, shared amortized network (e.g., a 2-layer MLP) will be trained to predict iterative updates to any encoder state vector. A learned halting module, integrated into each decoder layer, will output a Bernoulli gate at each generation step; a 'refine' signal triggers the amortized network to update the relevant encoder states based on the current decoder context. These refined states are cached for subsequent steps. Training will use a combination of task loss and a sparsity penalty on the halting module's gate, encouraging efficient refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a practical method for dynamic encoder refinement, making it viable for large-scale models. It will provide empirical evidence on tasks like machine translation, summarization, and semantic parsing that sparse, amortized refinement can outperform static encoders and rival more expensive dynamic baselines. The proposed system offers a new pathway for integrating iterative refinement and adaptive computation into standard autoregressive architectures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the combination of amortization (a single small network for all updates) and learned sparse triggering within the decoder loop, moving beyond prior work on ACT or universal transformers which typically apply dense, costly refinement at every step or layer.

proposals.title: Dynamic Encoder Refinement via Consistency Training and Natural Language Feedback
proposals.motivation: Transformer-based encoder-decoder models compute a static, bidirectional representation of the source sequence, which is then attended to by the decoder. This static bottleneck may limit the model's ability to refine its understanding of the source based on the evolving decoding context or external feedback, a key aspect of human-like iterative comprehension. Addressing this aligns with the researcher's themes of feedback and human-level processing in sequence tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that allowing encoder states to be dynamically refined during the decoding process, guided by consistency objectives and natural language feedback, will improve model performance on complex generation and reasoning tasks compared to static encoder representations.
proposals.method: We propose a training framework where a primary encoder-decoder model is augmented with a lightweight, iterative refinement module that updates encoder hidden states between decoder steps. This module will be trained using a consistency loss, ensuring refined states remain semantically faithful to the original input, inspired by consistency training methods. We will integrate natural language feedback signals, building on the researcher's prior work, to guide the refinement process in a human-in-the-loop or simulated feedback setting, treating the refinement trajectory as a sequence of 'subreads'.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture and training paradigm that breaks the static encoder bottleneck, enabling more adaptive sequence-to-sequence models. It will provide new insights into integrating iterative refinement and explicit feedback into the core attention mechanism of transformers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored iterative refinement in decoders (e.g., refinement networks) or used feedback for fine-tuning, dynamically updating the encoder's internal representations *during* the attention process based on decoding context and feedback is a non-obvious and unexplored direction for standard transformer models.

proposals.title: Contrastive Attentional Gating for Parallel, High-Fidelity Neural Speech Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current high-quality neural text-to-speech (TTS) systems, like Tacotron 2 or Transformer TTS, rely on autoregressive, left-to-right generation, which is inherently sequential and slow. While non-autoregressive models (e.g., FastSpeech) improve speed, they often require external alignment models and can suffer from over-smoothing or lack of fine acoustic detail, struggling to model the complex, long-range dependencies in speech. A method that achieves parallel generation with the fidelity and coherence of autoregressive models remains a significant open challenge in speech synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and suppressing the least predictive or 'irrelevant' past acoustic context for the current frame—via a novel contrastive attention mechanism—will provide a powerful, complementary signal that enables a single-pass, left-to-right model to generate high-fidelity, coherent speech in parallel.
proposals.method: We will develop a single-pass encoder-decoder architecture where the decoder, for each output acoustic frame, computes two parallel attention distributions over the encoded linguistic sequence: a standard 'salience' attention and a novel 'contrast' attention. The contrast attention will be trained via a consistency objective, such as a contrastive loss, to attend to past frames that are least predictive of the current target. The representations from these two attention heads will be fused via a gating mechanism (e.g., a learned sigmoid gate) to modulate the primary generation pathway in a Transformer or Conformer decoder block, refining the prediction in one forward pass.
proposals.contribution: This work aims to deliver a new neural TTS model that matches the quality of state-of-the-art autoregressive systems while operating in a fully parallel manner, significantly accelerating synthesis. It will contribute a novel architectural paradigm for sequence generation that redefines the utility of attention by making low-attention regions computationally valuable, potentially applicable beyond speech to other sequential domains.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit computation and utilization of 'contrastive' or anti-predictive context within a single-pass attention mechanism for refinement, moving beyond the standard paradigm where only salient features are aggregated. This approach is distinct from prior work on monotonic or hard alignments in non-autoregressive TTS.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Adaptive Location Priors for Cross-Domain Sequence Alignment
proposals.motivation: Effective sequence modeling in domains like protein alignment or gesture recognition relies on appropriate inductive biases about alignment structure, such as monotonicity or 2D locality. Manually designing these location priors for each new task is inefficient and suboptimal. A system that can automatically infer and adapt such structural constraints from limited data would greatly enhance the robustness and sample efficiency of sequence models across diverse applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework can acquire a shared, parameterized representation of location-aware attention that can be rapidly specialized to impose the correct structural prior (e.g., monotonic, 2D, hierarchical) for a new sequence modeling task, leading to superior cross-domain generalization compared to fixed-prior or from-scratch models.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML-like) system where the inner loop adapts a parameterized location-bias module. This module will generate biases for an attention mechanism (e.g., Transformer). The meta-training curriculum will consist of diverse tasks with known priors (e.g., machine translation for monotonicity, image captioning for 2D scans, RNA folding for hierarchy). The outer loop will optimize the initial parameters of the bias module so that a few gradient steps on a new task's support set yield an effective task-specific prior. We will evaluate on held-out domains like protein remote homology detection and continuous gesture recognition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven method for learning and transferring structural inductive biases in sequence models. It will yield a flexible attention mechanism that requires minimal target-domain examples to specialize, advancing few-shot learning for structured prediction tasks. The results will demonstrate the value of explicitly meta-learning alignment constraints.
proposals.novelty: While location-based attention and meta-learning are established separately, their combination to meta-learn *adaptive* structural priors for cross-domain sequence alignment is novel. This moves beyond using fixed biases or learning priors from scratch for each domain.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Memory Gist Networks: Adaptive Compression for Efficient Long-Context Transformers
proposals.motivation: Transformer models are fundamentally limited by the quadratic complexity of self-attention, restricting their ability to process very long sequences critical for tasks like book-level summarization, long-form dialogue, and scientific document analysis. Existing methods for long-context modeling, such as sparse attention or recurrence, often sacrifice fine-grained access to early context or struggle with information retention over extreme distances. A robust, adaptive mechanism for compressing and retrieving past context is needed to overcome these limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer augmented with a dynamic, hierarchical memory system that learns to compress early sequence segments into adaptive 'gist' representations will enable more effective and computationally efficient reasoning over sequences far longer than its training context window.
proposals.method: We will design a Hierarchical Memory Gist Network (HMGN) that integrates with a standard transformer encoder. The model will process the input stream in chunks, applying a learned compression function (a small, trainable network) to form a hierarchical tree of gist vectors, where higher-level nodes represent increasingly abstract summaries. A gist-retrieval module, using cross-attention between the current context and the memory tree, will dynamically decide which compressed representations to decompress and attend to for the current processing step. The entire system will be trained end-to-end using a consistency objective, ensuring that the model's outputs remain consistent whether it processes a sequence in one pass or via the chunked, compressed memory pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architecture for long-context modeling that explicitly optimizes the trade-off between memory footprint and contextual fidelity. It will advance methods for efficient sequence processing and offer a computational framework inspired by human memory hierarchies. The resulting models will be evaluated on long-document QA and multi-turn dialogue tasks to demonstrate superior length generalization.
proposals.novelty: While chunking and memory are established ideas, the novelty lies in the *adaptive, hierarchical, and learnable* nature of the compression, which is directly optimized via a consistency loss for downstream task performance rather than being a fixed heuristic. This integrates Cho's themes of feedback and human-level sequence processing into a concrete, trainable system.

proposals.title: Learning to Extrapolate: Sequence-Level Consistency Training for Robust Long-Context Language Models
proposals.motivation: Transformer-based language models exhibit performance degradation on input sequences significantly longer than those seen during training, limiting their application to long documents, multi-turn dialogues, and complex reasoning tasks. This brittleness stems from the fixed positional encodings and the lack of explicit training objectives for length generalization. Addressing this is crucial for building robust systems that can handle real-world, unbounded-length data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a model to produce consistent, length-invariant representations and outputs for the same semantic content across different sequence lengths will systematically improve its robustness to very long, out-of-distribution input sequences.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage training method. First, we adapt the consistency training framework, drawing inspiration from denoising diffusion models, to the sequence domain: we generate 'subreads' (truncated, overlapping segments) from long training sequences and enforce consistency between the model's internal representations for a token predicted from different subreads. Second, we integrate a sequence-level natural language feedback (NLF) loop, where a critic model provides feedback on the coherence and factual consistency of generations from truncated vs. full contexts, using this feedback as an auxiliary training signal to refine the primary model's long-context understanding.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training methodology for improving length extrapolation in autoregressive transformers. It will provide empirical evidence on the effectiveness of consistency and feedback-based objectives for this problem. The resulting models will demonstrate improved performance on long-context QA and summarization benchmarks without architectural changes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of consistency training—previously used in image generation and protein alignment—to the sequence length dimension, combined with a system-level NLF mechanism to provide a high-level, semantic training signal for long-range coherence, moving beyond local token prediction.

proposals.title: Dynamic Neural Lexicon: Joint Sub-Word Retrieval and Scoring for Open-Vocabulary ASR
proposals.motivation: Current large-vocabulary automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems face a lexical access bottleneck, struggling with out-of-vocabulary words and acoustic confusions in conversational speech. Scaling to truly open vocabularies requires moving beyond fixed pronunciation lexicons and direct phoneme-to-word mappings, which are brittle and limit generalization. A system that can dynamically access and score lexical candidates from a learned, compositional sub-word space is needed for robust performance on diverse, evolving language.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid ASR system with a neural lexicon module that performs dynamic, joint retrieval and scoring of sub-word units from a large learned inventory will outperform fixed-lexicon and end-to-end phoneme-based models on open-vocabulary tasks, particularly for unseen words and noisy acoustic conditions.
proposals.method: We propose a three-component architecture: 1) an acoustic encoder (e.g., Conformer) producing phoneme-like subreads, 2) a neural lexicon module implementing a differentiable key-value memory network, where keys are embeddings of learned sub-word units (e.g., morphs or byte-pair-encoding segments) and values are their corresponding acoustic embeddings, enabling dynamic candidate retrieval, and 3) a neural language model for sequence scoring. All components are trained jointly using a consistency-based objective, combining connectionist temporal classification (CTC) loss with a retrieval-augmented cross-entropy loss, ensuring the lexicon module learns acoustically grounded and linguistically plausible units.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable architecture for open-vocabulary ASR that explicitly addresses the lexical access problem. It will demonstrate how joint training of retrieval, acoustic modeling, and language modeling can improve generalization to out-of-vocabulary words and conversational speech. The resulting model and analysis of the learned sub-word inventory will offer insights into integrating discrete symbolic reasoning with neural sequence modeling.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the integration of a trainable, dynamic neural lexicon for retrieval and scoring within an end-to-end ASR framework, moving beyond static pronunciation dictionaries or monolithic sequence-to-sequence models. This approach uniquely combines ideas from memory-augmented neural networks, sub-word modeling, and joint acoustic-language training for lexical access, a direction less explored in pre-2026 ASR research.

proposals.title: Scaffolded Speech Recognition: Hierarchical Lexical Decoding via Subword Composition and Language Model Feedback
proposals.motivation: End-to-end speech recognition models struggle to generalize to rare or out-of-vocabulary words, as they must learn a monolithic mapping from acoustics to a vast, fixed word vocabulary. This limits scalability and robustness, especially for morphologically rich languages. Drawing an analogy from hierarchical genome assembly, where short reads are assembled into contigs using reference scaffolds, offers a promising path to a more compositional and data-efficient decoding strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical decoding system that first predicts reliable phoneme-level sequences, then composes them into words using a statistical language model defined over a learned inventory of subword units (e.g., morphemes, syllables), will improve generalization to unseen words and overall recognition accuracy compared to flat, word-level end-to-end models.
proposals.method: We will train a phoneme-level acoustic model using connectionist temporal classification (CTC) or an encoder-decoder architecture. Its output sequences will be treated as 'subreads.' A separate module, inspired by genome assembly overlap-layout-consensus methods, will compose these into candidate subword units (morphemes) using a learned inventory. A neural language model, operating on this subword vocabulary and trained with consistency objectives, will act as the 'reference scaffold' to assemble the final word sequence, providing iterative feedback to refine the acoustic predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, biologically-inspired architecture for speech recognition that explicitly separates acoustic modeling from lexical composition, improving interpretability and generalization. It will demonstrate how subword-level language models can provide effective feedback for refining acoustic predictions, advancing the theme of system-level natural language feedback.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct adaptation of hierarchical assembly concepts from bioinformatics (k-mers, contigs, scaffolds) to the speech recognition decoding problem, framing subword units as the fundamental building blocks for lexical construction, guided by a language model acting as a scaffold.

proposals.title: Scaling Subword Consistency Training for Large-Vocabulary Continuous Speech Recognition via Human-in-the-Loop Feedback
