proposals.motivation: End-to-end speech recognition models struggle with large-vocabulary, word-level tasks due to data inefficiency and error propagation from phoneme or subword units to meaningful words. Pre-2026, methods like consistency training and subreads have shown promise for structured sequence tasks, but scaling them to open-vocabulary speech recognition remains an open challenge. Integrating natural language feedback at the system level, as explored in recent text-based work, could provide a novel learning signal to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training framework combining subword-level consistency regularization with iterative, system-level natural language feedback from human or simulated users can significantly improve the sample efficiency and final accuracy of end-to-end models on large-vocabulary continuous speech recognition.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based encoder-decoder ASR model initially trained on phoneme recognition. First, we will apply consistency training at the subword (BPE) level, enforcing model predictions to be invariant to acoustic perturbations and subword segmentation variants ('subreads'). Second, we will implement a feedback loop where model transcripts are presented to a human (or a pre-2026 LLM-based simulator) that provides corrective natural language instructions (e.g., 'merge X and Y into word Z'). This feedback will be encoded and used to fine-tune the model via a gradient-based update, aligning the decoding process with word-level correctness.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training paradigm for LVCSR that integrates low-level acoustic consistency with high-level semantic feedback. We will release a benchmark for feedback-augmented speech recognition and demonstrate improved performance on LibriSpeech, particularly in limited-data regimes. The research will provide insights into how human-centric feedback can be operationalized for complex sequence transduction tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of subword-level consistency methods (inspired by protein/radiation work) with system-level natural language feedback (inspired by recent NLP work) for speech recognition, creating a multi-level learning framework not explored in pre-2026 ASR literature.

proposals.title: Length-Agnostic Neural Speech Turing Machines for Robust Long-Form Context Tracking
proposals.motivation: Current speech processing models, including end-to-end ASR and dialogue systems, struggle with long-form audio due to fixed-length context windows and sensitivity to train-test length mismatch. This limits their robustness in real-world applications like extended meetings, audiobooks, or multi-turn conversations where maintaining a consistent, long-range context is crucial. A model that can dynamically manage an external memory of acoustic-linguistic features, independent of absolute input length, is needed to address these fundamental scalability and generalization challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fusing a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) architecture with length-agnostic training principles will yield a speech processing model whose performance on context-dependent tasks (e.g., dialogue state tracking, coreference resolution in speech) is robust to extreme variations in input segment length, both during training and at test time.
proposals.method: We will develop a model with an encoder that extracts frame-level acoustic features, coupled with an NTM-style external memory matrix. The model will be trained using a curriculum of varied-length speech segments (inspired by 'subreads' and consistency training methods). A key innovation is a length-agnostic attention mechanism for memory access, where read/write operations are conditioned on content-based addressing and a learned summary state, not absolute positional indices. The training objective will combine a primary task loss (e.g., next-utterance prediction, named entity tracking) with an auxiliary consistency loss that enforces stability of memory content summaries across different segmentations of the same long sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel architecture for long-form speech processing that decouples model capacity from input length via dynamic external memory, 2) a demonstration of length-agnostic training for speech, improving robustness to length distribution shifts, and 3) new benchmarks and analysis for long-range dependency tracking in continuous speech, moving beyond text-based context models.
proposals.novelty: While NTMs and memory networks exist, their application to raw or featurized speech with an explicit length-agnostic training objective is novel. The fusion specifically targets the unique challenges of continuous, variable-rate speech signals, differing from prior work on discrete text sequences or fixed-length audio windows.

proposals.title: Globally-Coherent Sequence-to-Sequence Learning via Energy-Based Structured Attention
proposals.motivation: Standard dot-product attention in Transformers models token interactions independently, lacking explicit mechanisms for globally coherent alignments, which is crucial for tasks like machine translation and protein structure prediction. Structured attention models, such as those using Conditional Random Fields (CRFs), can capture these dependencies but suffer from sequential inference bottlenecks (e.g., the Viterbi algorithm), making them intractable for modern long-sequence tasks. A scalable method for globally-structured attention is needed to improve model consistency without sacrificing computational efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural energy-based model (EBM), defining a Gibbs distribution over latent attention alignments, can capture pairwise or higher-order dependencies while enabling parallel, tractable training and inference via noise-contrastive estimation (NCE) or continuous relaxations of dynamic programming.
proposals.method: We will design a structured attention layer where a neural EBM defines an energy function over potential alignment matrices, parameterized to capture local and long-range constraints. For training, we will employ Noise-Contrastive Estimation (NCE) with a proposal distribution from a standard attention mechanism, or a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation to enable gradient-based learning through a dynamic programming backbone. This approach will be integrated into an encoder-decoder framework and evaluated on machine translation (WMT) and remote protein homology detection tasks, benchmarking against standard Transformers and exact CRF-based attention.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel attention mechanism that provides globally-coherent structured predictions while maintaining parallelizable training and sub-quadratic inference complexity. It will advance the field of structured prediction in deep learning by demonstrating how energy-based models can be scaled to sequence-to-sequence tasks. The resulting models are expected to show improved performance on tasks requiring precise structural alignment, such as protein remote homology detection and low-resource machine translation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is replacing the exact, sequential CRF with a trainable neural energy model for attention, enabling the capture of complex dependencies without the inference bottleneck. This contrasts with prior structured attention work that relied on exact inference or locally-factorized approximations, and aligns with pre-2026 explorations of EBMs for discrete structures but applies them specifically to the core attention operation in sequence models.

proposals.title: Structured Attention: Modeling Alignment Dependencies in Sequence-to-Sequence Learning with Conditional Random Fields
proposals.motivation: Standard neural attention mechanisms in sequence-to-sequence models compute alignment weights independently at each decoder step, lacking an explicit model for the temporal dependencies between these alignments. This can lead to attention patterns that are erratic or non-monotonic, which is problematic for tasks like machine translation where smooth, monotonic attention is linguistically plausible. A principled framework to incorporate such structural priors into attention could improve model consistency and interpretability, especially for low-resource or structured prediction tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the sequence of attention alignments as a structured output using a Conditional Random Field (CRF), we can learn more accurate and linguistically plausible attention patterns that respect dependencies across decoding steps, leading to improved translation quality and model robustness.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CRF framework from Gene Regulatory Network inference to model the conditional probability of an alignment sequence given encoder and decoder states. The emission potentials will be derived from standard attention scores (e.g., dot-product or MLP-based), while transition potentials will encode priors for smoothness or monotonicity. Training will involve maximizing the conditional log-likelihood of reference alignments (derived from forced decoding) or marginalizing over alignments during end-to-end sequence-level training, using dynamic programming for efficient inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, probabilistic method for injecting structural biases into neural attention, moving beyond local, step-wise computation. It will yield a more interpretable and controllable attention mechanism, potentially improving performance on tasks requiring consistent alignment. The project also creates a formal bridge between structured prediction techniques in computational biology and core problems in deep learning for NLP.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are established for sequence labeling, their application to directly model the internal, latent alignment structure of a neural sequence-to-sequence model's attention mechanism is novel. This approach fundamentally re-frames attention as a structured prediction problem rather than an independent scoring mechanism.

proposals.title: Structured Attention: Modeling Alignment Dependencies in Sequence-to-Sequence Learning with Conditional Random Fields
proposals.motivation: In neural machine translation and related sequence-to-sequence tasks, the attention mechanism computes alignments independently at each timestep, ignoring natural dependencies between alignments across time. This can lead to incoherent or jittery attention maps, which may degrade translation fluency and harm model interpretability. Modeling these dependencies is a core structured prediction problem that remains underexplored in standard attention-based architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a Conditional Random Field (CRF) layer to model dependencies between attention alignments will produce more coherent and linguistically plausible attention distributions, leading to improved translation consistency and final output quality.
proposals.method: We will augment a standard encoder-decoder model with an attention CRF. The encoder's hidden states and previous decoder state will provide input features for a linear-chain CRF that operates over the discrete space of source positions at each decoder step. The CRF's pairwise potentials will encourage smoothness and linguistically valid transitions (e.g., monotonic or skip patterns). Training will involve integrating the CRF marginal inference into the attention probability computation, using backpropagation through the dynamic programming algorithms for the CRF. We will benchmark on WMT translation tasks and structured prediction tasks like syntactic parsing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, probabilistic framework for structured attention, moving beyond local, independent decisions. It will yield more interpretable alignment matrices that better reflect linguistic structure. The integration demonstrates how classic structured prediction methods can enhance modern neural architectures, potentially improving robustness and sample efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are well-established for output sequence modeling, applying them explicitly to model the internal structure of the attention mechanism—treating the alignment path as a latent structured variable—is a novel direction. This differs from prior work on differentiable pattern constraints or Gaussian smoothing for attention.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Length-Adaptive Alignment Policies via Reinforced Attention
proposals.motivation: Sequence alignment is a fundamental task in NLP and computational biology, but existing models (e.g., standard attention or dynamic time warping) often require fixed-length inputs or expensive retraining for new length distributions. This limits their application to real-world data with extreme length variability, such as protein sequences of remote homology or long conversational transcripts. A robust, length-agnostic alignment method would significantly improve generalization and efficiency across domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning agent can meta-learn a policy to dynamically reconfigure an attention mechanism's search strategy based on the length and structure of an input sequence, enabling efficient, accurate alignment without architectural changes or retraining for new length distributions.
proposals.method: We will design a Transformer-based architecture where a policy network, trained via a model-free RL algorithm like PPO, outputs parameters (e.g., sparsity patterns, window sizes, or scaling factors) for a modified attention mechanism conditioned on sequence-level features. The RL agent's reward will combine alignment accuracy (e.g., via differentiable dynamic programming) and computational efficiency. Training will use a curriculum of tasks with progressively more diverse sequence lengths, enforcing a consistency objective to stabilize learning across length regimes, inspired by subread and consistency training methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a single, general-purpose model capable of aligning sequences of arbitrary and unseen lengths, reducing the need for task-specific engineering. It will advance the integration of meta-learning and RL for adaptive neural architectures, providing a concrete framework for length-robust sequence processing. The resulting system will be evaluated on challenging benchmarks in protein remote homology detection and system-level natural language feedback alignment.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop, RL-driven adaptation of attention's internal search strategy as a function of input sequence properties, moving beyond fixed or heuristically sparse patterns. This creates a synergy between transfer RL for policy learning and length-agnostic architectural design, a combination not explored in pre-2026 alignment literature.

proposals.title: Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Attention Paths in Sequence-to-Sequence Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current attention mechanisms in sequence models operate via fixed, feed-forward computations, which may be suboptimal for complex alignment tasks like structural protein homology or non-monotonic machine translation. These tasks require exploring multiple potential alignment paths, a process not naturally supported by standard soft attention. Reinforcement learning (RL) offers a principled framework for learning a policy to explore such discrete alignment decisions, potentially improving model robustness and interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an attention mechanism with a reinforcement learning objective, where the agent's actions are discrete choices of alignment paths, will yield more accurate and generalizable alignments for challenging sequence-to-sequence tasks compared to standard trained attention.
proposals.method: We will frame the alignment search as a Markov Decision Process where, at each step, a policy network (a lightweight controller) selects a candidate alignment focus (e.g., a source token or a segment). The core sequence model (an encoder-decoder with a modified attention module) will use this selection. We will employ an actor-critic RL algorithm, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), with a reward based on task-specific performance (e.g., BLEU score or structural similarity) and a consistency regularization term to stabilize learning. The policy will be pre-trained via behavioral cloning on suboptimal alignments from a standard model (subreads) before fine-tuning with RL.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, RL-driven attention mechanism that actively searches for alignment paths. It will provide a new methodology for integrating RL into core neural network components for structured prediction. The resulting models are expected to demonstrate improved performance on alignment-heavy tasks like protein remote homology detection and non-monotonic translation.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been used for overall sequence generation, applying it specifically to train the internal attention mechanism to explore alignment paths is a novel direction. This moves beyond using RL for discrete output selection to using it for guiding the internal computational pathway of the model itself.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Attention Networks for Multi-Scale Speech Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current end-to-end speech models often learn monolithic representations, obscuring the rich hierarchical structure of speech (phonemes, words, phrases) crucial for human-like understanding and robust generalization. Explicitly modeling these levels, as done in document-level NLP with hierarchical attention, remains underexplored for speech, limiting model interpretability and performance on tasks requiring compositional reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical attention model that explicitly attends to syllable, word, and phrase levels in speech can learn more interpretable and compositionally structured representations, leading to improved performance and data efficiency on downstream tasks like spoken language understanding and low-resource speech recognition.
proposals.method: We will adapt the hierarchical attention network (HAN) architecture from document classification to the speech domain. The model will process raw audio or low-level features using a convolutional encoder. The first attention layer will attend to frames within learned syllable-like segments. These segment embeddings will be aggregated via a second attention layer to form word-level representations, which are then similarly aggregated via a third attention layer to form phrase-level representations. We will train this model using a multi-task objective combining connectionist temporal classification (CTC) loss for alignment and a sequence-level consistency loss (inspired by Cho's recent work) to enforce coherence across hierarchical levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel neural architecture for multi-scale speech processing, yielding representations that are more aligned with linguistic structure. It will offer new insights into how hierarchical compositionality can be induced in speech models, potentially improving performance on tasks like intent detection from audio and code-switching speech recognition. The model's attention maps will also serve as an interpretable diagnostic tool.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical attention is established in NLP, its principled application to the continuous, weakly segmented speech signal is non-trivial and novel. The integration of sequence-level consistency training to regularize the interactions between the learned speech sub-reads (syllables, words) moves beyond standard supervised training paradigms.

proposals.title: Inverse Attention for End-to-End Neural Speech Synthesis with Natural Language Feedback
proposals.motivation: Current neural text-to-speech (TTS) systems, while powerful, often rely on complex multi-stage pipelines (e.g., mel-spectrogram prediction followed by a separate vocoder) or autoregressive decoding, which can be slow and prone to error propagation. A truly end-to-end, non-autoregressive model that directly maps text or phoneme sequences to raw waveform samples could improve efficiency and naturalness. This aligns with the researcher's interest in sequence-to-sequence modeling and system-level feedback for iterative refinement.
proposals.hypothesis: An attention-based encoder-decoder architecture, inspired by machine translation but applied in reverse to generate a continuous waveform from a discrete sequence, can achieve high-quality, efficient, and controllable speech synthesis when trained with consistency objectives and refined using natural language feedback.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based encoder-decoder, treating the phoneme/text sequence as the 'source' and the raw waveform as the 'target.' The decoder will generate the waveform in a non-autoregressive manner using a latent representation, inspired by diffusion or flow-based models for continuous data. Key innovations include: 1) using a 'subreads' mechanism to allow the decoder to attend to overlapping, multi-scale chunks of the encoded sequence for better prosody, 2) applying consistency training techniques to stabilize the generation of long, high-fidelity audio sequences, and 3) integrating a feedback loop where a critic model (trained on human preferences) provides natural language corrections to iteratively improve synthesis quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, fully end-to-end architecture for neural TTS that eliminates intermediate representations. It will demonstrate how training techniques like consistency models and feedback mechanisms, successful in other domains, can be applied to the challenging inverse problem of direct waveform synthesis. The resulting system aims to set a new benchmark for synthesis speed and controllability.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual 'inversion' of the standard NLP attention paradigm to generate a high-dimensional continuous signal (audio) from a discrete sequence, combined with the application of consistency training and human-level natural language feedback specifically for raw waveform generation, which is largely unexplored in pre-2026 TTS research.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Acoustic and Alignment Consistency via Sequence-Level Feedback Training
proposals.motivation: End-to-end multilingual speech recognition models often underperform on low-resource languages due to insufficient data and fail to leverage shared linguistic structures across languages effectively. Pre-2026, methods for cross-lingual knowledge transfer in attention-based models are limited, and training dynamics often lead to language interference rather than synergy. This work addresses the core challenge of building a single, efficient model that robustly recognizes speech in multiple languages by sharing acoustic and alignment representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a single attention-based encoder-decoder model with a novel sequence-level consistency objective, driven by human-like feedback on alignment quality, will force the model to learn language-invariant acoustic features and a unified alignment space, improving performance on all languages, especially low-resource ones.
proposals.method: We will extend a Transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture for speech recognition. The core innovation is a two-stage training method: first, we pre-train the encoder on multiple languages using a contrastive loss on multilingual subreads (inspired by subword regularization) to encourage acoustic unit sharing. Second, we introduce a consistency training loop where the model's cross-attention alignments for an utterance are evaluated by a separately trained 'feedback' module (a small critic network) that predicts alignment plausibility scores; the model is then fine-tuned to maximize these scores, enforcing linguistically coherent alignments across languages.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new training framework for multilingual ASR that explicitly optimizes for shared acoustic and alignment representations through a learned consistency objective. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of sequence-level feedback for cross-lingual transfer and release a strong single-model baseline for multilingual speech recognition benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating a trainable, sequence-level feedback mechanism—inspired by Cho's work on system-level NL feedback—directly into the multilingual ASR training loop to supervise alignment consistency, moving beyond simple multi-task or adversarial training schemes common pre-2026.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Cross-Lingual Alignment via Feedback-Driven Consistency Training
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised neural machine translation and cross-lingual representation learning often rely on heuristics like iterative back-translation or adversarial alignment, which can be unstable and require careful tuning. The core challenge of learning semantic correspondences between languages without any parallel data remains open. This research addresses a fundamental bottleneck in scaling multilingual NLP to low-resource language pairs where parallel corpora are unavailable.
proposals.hypothesis: A sequence-to-sequence model can learn implicit, useful cross-lingual alignments by optimizing for consistency between its own generations and the source input, guided by a system-level natural language feedback loop, without ever seeing a parallel sentence pair.
proposals.method: We will train a single multilingual Transformer encoder-decoder on non-parallel monolingual corpora from two languages. The core training objective will enforce consistency: the model encodes a source sentence, decodes a translation, re-encodes this generation, and must reconstruct the original source sentence. A feedback module, inspired by 'System-Level Natural Language Feedback', will analyze the reconstruction error and generate a scalar reward or a natural language instruction to adjust the latent alignment path. We will adapt denoising and consistency training techniques from recent generative models (e.g., denoising U-Nets) to stabilize this cycle, treating the alignment as a latent variable refined through iterative sub-reads of the sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for purely unsupervised alignment learning, moving beyond statistical heuristics. It will yield insights into how feedback and consistency constraints can induce structured latent representations in seq2seq models. The resulting model could serve as a stronger baseline for unsupervised translation and cross-lingual transfer tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior unsupervised methods that rely on distribution matching or back-translation, this approach integrates an explicit, learnable feedback mechanism to guide alignment discovery directly within the sequence generation loop, framing alignment as a consistency optimization problem solvable through self-supervision.

proposals.title: Learning Hard Monotonic Alignments with Differentiable Feedback
proposals.motivation: Standard soft attention mechanisms in sequence-to-sequence models produce dense, often diffuse, alignment distributions, which can be difficult to interpret and may not reflect the discrete, often monotonic, alignments found in tasks like phoneme recognition, morphological inflection, or certain forms of program synthesis. Enforcing sparsity or hardness in attention could improve model interpretability, data efficiency, and inductive bias for such structured tasks, aligning with the researcher's interest in human-level feedback and system-level constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a differentiable, feedback-driven training objective that enforces hard, sparse attention—potentially with monotonic constraints—we can improve the alignment quality, sample efficiency, and interpretability of sequence models without sacrificing end-to-end differentiability.
proposals.method: We will extend the standard attention mechanism with a trainable, discrete selection process. Specifically, we will employ a Gumbel-Softmax or Sparsemax transformation to obtain sparse, near-hard attention distributions during training. To enforce monotonicity and further sparsity, we will incorporate a consistency loss based on the entropy of the attention distribution and a penalty for deviations from a monotonic path, inspired by pre-2026 work on monotonic attention and neural alignment. The training will use a feedback loop where the sharpness of the alignment is iteratively refined based on task performance, similar to sub-reads or consistency training methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, fully differentiable method for learning hard, interpretable alignments within standard encoder-decoder frameworks. It will contribute a new perspective on integrating discrete structural constraints into neural models via feedback, potentially improving performance on tasks requiring precise alignment.
proposals.novelty: While sparse and monotonic attention exist, our approach uniquely combines differentiable hardness with a feedback-driven consistency objective to *learn* the appropriate sparsity pattern directly from task-level supervision, moving beyond fixed or heuristic sparsification methods.

proposals.title: Phrase-Level Soft Alignment via Hierarchical Consistency Training for Sequence-to-Sequence Models
proposals.motivation: Current neural sequence-to-sequence models, particularly in machine translation and text generation, rely on word-level attention or soft-searching mechanisms. This granularity fails to capture the compositional semantics of multi-word phrases, leading to issues with idiomatic expression translation and coherent long-form generation. Extending alignment to the phrase level is a critical step towards more human-like, structurally-aware language processing, as highlighted by recent work on system-level natural language feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical alignment model, which learns to softly search over both words and learned phrase chunks, will improve the semantic consistency and structural fidelity of generated sequences compared to standard word-level models.
proposals.method: We will extend the standard Transformer architecture by integrating a two-level alignment module. The lower level performs standard word-to-word attention. The higher level operates on dynamically segmented phrase chunks, generated via a lightweight, differentiable segmentation network inspired by subword regularization techniques. A novel consistency loss will be applied between the two levels, enforcing that the phrase-level alignment is a coherent aggregation of word-level signals, similar to the consistency objectives used in denoising models. Training will involve joint optimization of translation/ generation loss, segmentation loss, and the hierarchical consistency loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, general-purpose architecture for phrase-aware sequence transduction. It will provide empirical evidence on machine translation and text summarization tasks that phrase-level alignment improves BLEU, ROUGE, and human evaluation scores for coherence. The method also offers a new perspective on integrating discrete linguistic structures (phrases) into fully differentiable neural models.
proposals.novelty: While subword units are common, explicitly modeling soft, learnable phrase chunks within the alignment mechanism of a Transformer is novel. The key innovation is the hierarchical consistency training that binds discrete-seeming phrase structures to continuous word-level attention, moving beyond fixed n-gram or rule-based phrase tables.

proposals.title: Learning from Subword-Level Human Feedback for Robust Open-Vocabulary Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation (NMT) models struggle with rare and out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words, often resorting to copying or producing nonsensical translations, which degrades reliability in real-world applications. This limitation stems from fixed subword vocabularies and a training objective that lacks explicit, fine-grained feedback on how to decompose and translate novel lexical units. Addressing this is crucial for models to generalize beyond their training corpora and handle evolving language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that providing sequence-level natural language feedback, grounded in subword-level error analysis from human annotators, will enable NMT models to learn more robust decomposition and translation strategies for rare and unseen source words.
proposals.method: We will extend the feedback learning framework from 'System-Level Natural Language Feedback' to operate on subword sequences. A denoising U-Net architecture, inspired by the listed cosmology work, will be adapted as a 'subread' critic model to predict the quality of potential subword segmentations of an OOV word. This critic will be trained on human feedback pairs (source OOV, translation attempt, corrective feedback). The NMT model will be trained with a consistency loss, aligning its encoder's subword representations with the critic's quality scores, encouraging it to generate segmentations that lead to correct translations as judged by the feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for integrating fine-grained human feedback into NMT training, moving beyond the sentence level. It will yield a model with improved robustness to lexical novelty and provide a new benchmark for evaluating OOV translation, linking human-in-the-loop learning with architectural inductive biases for subword structure.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit use of human feedback to train a model's internal subword segmentation policy for translation, combining sequence-level feedback learning with a denoising-based critic for subword 'consistency', an approach not explored in prior OOV handling techniques like byte-pair encoding or copy mechanisms.

proposals.title: Coherent Long-Form Translation via Hierarchical Feedback and Consistency Training
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation (NMT) excels at sentence-level tasks but struggles with document-level coherence, failing to maintain consistent terminology, style, and discourse structure across paragraphs. This limitation hinders the practical application of NMT to real-world documents, technical manuals, and literature. Addressing this requires new architectures and training paradigms that operate beyond the short-sequence context of standard Transformers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical encoder-decoder model, trained with a novel consistency objective on sub-reads of the long output and guided by system-level natural language feedback, can significantly improve translation coherence and accuracy for paragraph-length inputs.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Transformer architecture with a hierarchical encoder: a lower-level encoder processes individual sentences, and a higher-level encoder (modeled after a document-level denoising U-Net) integrates cross-sentence context. The decoder will generate text autoregressively. Training will involve two novel objectives: (1) A **consistency loss** that enforces agreement between the model's full-document output and its own translations of random, overlapping sub-sequences ('subreads') of the source, and (2) A **feedback loop** where a critic module (trained on human preferences) provides natural language feedback on coherence, which is encoded and used to condition the decoder during refinement steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable architecture and training methodology for long-form NMT, moving beyond sentence-level modeling. It will provide empirical evidence on the effectiveness of consistency training and learned feedback for document-level tasks, offering a new benchmark for coherent translation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of a hierarchical U-Net-inspired context encoder with consistency training on subreads and integrable natural language feedback—a fusion of Cho's core themes not previously applied to long-sequence translation.

proposals.title: Inverse Attention: Diagnosing and Improving Neural Machine Translation via Bidirectional Alignment Consistency
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation (NMT) models generate fluent outputs, but their internal alignment mechanisms—how source words map to target words—are often opaque and treated as a byproduct. This lack of interpretable, controllable alignment limits our ability to diagnose translation errors, provide targeted feedback, and ensure semantic faithfulness. Analyzing the inverse mapping from target back to source offers a novel lens for model introspection and improvement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a well-calibrated NMT model should exhibit high consistency between its standard (source-to-target) attention and a learned inverse (target-to-source) alignment; significant divergence between these mappings will pinpoint specific translation errors and areas for model refinement.
proposals.method: We will extend a standard Transformer-based NMT architecture by training a parallel, parameter-tied inverse attention module that predicts source word positions from the decoder's hidden states. Using the same training data, we will enforce a consistency loss (e.g., a symmetrized KL divergence) between the forward and inverse attention distributions for each sentence pair. This approach draws on concepts of consistency training and reversible architectures, akin to denoising objectives in other domains, to create a self-supervised feedback loop for alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new diagnostic tool for NMT, enabling fine-grained error analysis via alignment inconsistency. It will also demonstrate that explicitly modeling bidirectional alignment can serve as an auxiliary training objective, potentially improving translation robustness and faithfulness, especially for long-range dependencies and rare words.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses attention for visualization, we propose to actively learn and utilize the inverse alignment as a core component of the model's training and analysis, creating a symmetric, self-consistent alignment system rather than treating attention as a unidirectional, post-hoc feature.

proposals.title: Bidirectional Joint Alignment and Translation with Consistency Feedback
proposals.motivation: Current neural machine translation models are typically trained for a single translation direction, leading to inconsistencies between forward and backward models. This is suboptimal for tasks requiring symmetry, such as bilingual dictionary induction or iterative refinement. A single model performing both directions could enforce consistency, improve data efficiency, and provide a stronger internal representation of language alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: A single neural model, trained with a joint objective for bidirectional translation and alignment, will learn more consistent and higher-quality latent cross-lingual representations than independently trained unidirectional models, as measured by BLEU scores and alignment accuracy metrics.
proposals.method: We propose a Transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture with a shared encoder and two language-specific decoders. The model will be trained on parallel corpora with a multi-task objective combining: 1) standard cross-entropy losses for source-to-target and target-to-source translation, 2) a novel consistency loss that minimizes the divergence between the encoder's representations of a sentence pair and its reverse, inspired by denoising and consistency training methods. We will explore integrating explicit, differentiable alignment submodules (e.g., attention-based soft alignments) into the shared encoder, regularized by the bidirectional translation feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified model capable of high-quality bidirectional translation and soft alignment. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of symmetry and consistency in cross-lingual representation learning. The model and its learned alignments could serve as a stronger foundation for downstream multilingual tasks.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task and bidirectional training exist, jointly modeling explicit alignment and translation in both directions within a single, consistency-constrained architecture is unexplored. The approach uniquely leverages translation as a form of human-level feedback to refine alignment, and vice-versa, creating a closed-loop learning system.

ideas.title: Extend AdapterFusion to sequence generation tasks
ideas.description: Apply the AdapterFusion method to sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation or summarization.
ideas.sourceTitle: AdapterFusion: Non-Destructive Task Composition for Transfer Learning

ideas.title: Explore alternative RNN units for the encoder-decoder
ideas.description: Replace the simple tanh RNN with more sophisticated units to improve learning of long-range dependencies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Phrase Representations using RNN Encoder–Decoder for Statistical Machine Translation

ideas.title: Length-Agnostic Alignment Search via Reinforced Attention
ideas.description: This research combines transfer reinforcement learning (RL) with length-agnostic architecture design to create a meta-learning system for alignment search. The RL agent is trained not on a fixed sequence length but to adapt the attention mechanism's search strategy to the specific length and structure of an input sequence. This enables a single model to efficiently discover alignment paths for data with highly variable utterance or sequence lengths without retraining or architectural changes. The synergy yields a robust, general-purpose alignment search engine that transfers its learned length-adaptive policy to novel and complex length distributions at test time.

ideas.title: Structured Neural Energy Models for Efficient, Globally-Coherent Attention
ideas.description: This redesigned system replaces the exact CRF with a neural energy-based model that defines a Gibbs distribution over attention alignments, capturing pairwise or higher-order dependencies. The model is trained using a scalable objective like noise-contrastive estimation or a continuous relaxation of dynamic programming, enabling parallel computation and avoiding the sequential inference bottleneck. This maintains the original goal of structured attention modeling while achieving tractable training and inference on long sequences, making it viable for modern sequence-to-sequence tasks.

ideas.title: Learn alignment without explicit annotation
ideas.description: Investigate if the model can learn useful alignments without any parallel sentence data at all.
ideas.sourceTitle: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate

ideas.title: Scale to multilingual and cross-lingual recognition

ideas.title: Analyze convolutional and recurrent architectures

ideas.title: Adapting Gene Regulatory Network Inference to Model Attention Dependencies with CRFs
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from Computational Biology, specifically the problem of Gene Regulatory Network (GRN) inference. In GRN inference, the goal is to model the complex conditional dependencies between the expression states of multiple genes across experiments or time. This is often solved using structured prediction models like Conditional Random Fields (CRFs), which are ideal for modeling the conditional probability of a label sequence (gene states) given an observation sequence (expression data), capturing the network structure without modeling the joint distribution of all inputs. We adapt this solution to the context of neural attention mechanisms. Here, the 'observation sequence' is the encoder states and decoder state, and the 'label sequence' to be predicted is the sequence of attention alignments (e.g., which encoder position the decoder attends to at each step). By applying a CRF over these alignment variables, we can explicitly model desirable dependencies between attention weights across time—such as encouraging smooth, contiguous, or monotonic attention paths—which are often implicit or weakly enforced in standard attention mechanisms. This structured prediction approach provides a principled probabilistic framework to incorporate prior knowledge about attention behavior directly into the model's objective.

ideas.title: Neural Lexical Access for Open-Vocabulary Speech Recognition
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid speech recognition system designed to solve the lexical access bottleneck in scaling to large vocabularies. Instead of a direct phoneme-to-word mapping, the model uses a neural lexicon module that dynamically retrieves and scores candidates from a large, learned inventory of sub-word units. This module is jointly trained with the acoustic model and a neural language model to handle out-of-vocabulary words and acoustic confusions robustly. The resulting architecture enables accurate, large-vocabulary continuous speech recognition with strong generalization to unseen words and conversational speech.

ideas.title: Learned Latent-Temporal Feature Recovery via Auxiliary Attention Guidance
ideas.description: This approach augments a transformer-based language model with a parallel, lightweight Temporal Relevance Critic network that processes the same sequence. The critic is trained with an auxiliary objective to identify temporal segments whose features, when emphasized, improve prediction of a held-out or future context, creating a signal for what is 'relevantly irrelevant.' The base model's attention mechanism is then softly guided, via a gating mechanism, to increase focus on these critic-identified segments, enabling the discovery of non-obvious temporal dependencies without forcing attention onto pure noise.

ideas.title: Temporal Attention via Predictive Coding and Surprise Minimization
ideas.description: This idea inverts temporal attention focus by drawing an analogy from predictive processing in computational neuroscience. The analogous solution involves the brain using top-down predictions to highlight surprising prediction errors for learning. Adapted to language modeling, we propose a framework where the model first makes a lightweight prediction for an upcoming temporal context. The core attention mechanism is then biased or gated to attend more strongly to the parts of the actual input that most strongly violate this prediction—the segments deemed irrelevant by a standard predictive model. This forces the model to re-evaluate and learn from temporal surprises, potentially improving robustness, handling of rare events, and learning from counterfactual or anomalous narrative turns.

ideas.title: Extend neural operator framework analysis
ideas.description: Further develop the neural operator framework to analyze and generate other novel recurrent network architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: How to Construct Deep Recurrent Neural Networks

ideas.title: Adaptive Memory Compression for Long Sequences via Hierarchical Chunking
ideas.description: This research idea aims to improve model robustness to very long input sequences by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and human memory systems. The analogous problem is how the brain manages lifelong streams of information, solved through hierarchical chunking and gist extraction. The adapted solution is to design a transformer model with a dynamic, hierarchical compression mechanism that learns to form and store compressed 'gist' representations of early sequence segments. These gists are then selectively retrieved and decompressed via cross-attention when processing later context, enabling the model to handle sequences significantly longer than those seen in training while managing computational complexity.

ideas.title: Single-Pass Speech Generation via Attentional Contrast
ideas.description: This research explores a novel single-pass, left-to-right acoustic model for speech synthesis that fundamentally redefines attention. Instead of only attending to the most salient past features, the model is trained to explicitly compute a representation of the temporally 'irrelevant' or least predictive context for the current frame. This 'contrastive attention' signal is then used to modulate the primary generation pathway, effectively helping the model rule out implausible acoustic trajectories and refine its prediction in one forward pass. The approach challenges autoregressive dependency by making non-obvious, low-attention segments computationally valuable for parallel, high-fidelity generation.

ideas.title: Amortized and Sparse Dynamic Refinement of Encoder Representations for Attention
ideas.description: This system overcomes the prohibitive cost of naive dynamic refinement by using a small, amortized network to predict iterative updates to encoder states. These updates are triggered sparsely by a learned halting module in the decoder, which identifies steps where refined encoder context is most beneficial. The refined states are cached and reused, maintaining the benefits of dynamic representation while keeping computational overhead minimal and practical for large-scale models.

ideas.title: Make attention mechanism sparse or hard

ideas.sourceTitle: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate

ideas.title: Temporal Convolutional Attention for Counterfactual Sequence Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel attention mechanism that applies learnable convolutional filters across the temporal dimension of an input sequence (e.g., text, time-series, video frames) to generate feature maps highlighting multi-scale periodic or phased patterns. These convolutional feature maps are then used to dynamically re-weight the attention distribution, forcing the model to attend to temporal segments that standard self-attention deems low-relevance. The synergy creates a model that does not just passively re-weight attention but actively learns convolutional kernels optimized to extract predictive signals from 'irrelevant' temporal contexts, fundamentally changing how the model perceives sequential structure. This approach could uncover latent, non-local dependencies in language, sensor data, or historical event sequences that are invisible to standard transformer architectures.

ideas.title: Use reinforcement learning for discrete sequence generation

ideas.title: Meta-Learning Task-Adaptive Location Priors for Cross-Domain Sequence Modeling
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-learning system that learns to adapt location-aware attention mechanisms to the specific structural needs of diverse sequence tasks. Instead of direct application, the model is trained on a curriculum of tasks with varied location priors (monotonic, 2D, hierarchical) to learn a shared, parameterized location-bias module. The system can then rapidly specialize this module for a new target task, such as protein sequence alignment or gesture recognition, by fine-tuning it with a few examples to capture the new domain's unique alignment structure. This approach ensures robust cross-domain performance by explicitly learning how to infer and impose appropriate location constraints.

ideas.title: Length-Agnostic Neural Speech Turing Machines for Robust Long-Form Context Tracking
ideas.description: This research fuses the dynamic memory access of Neural Turing Machines with the principle of length-agnostic training to create a novel speech processing model. The core innovation is an external memory module that learns to read, write, and summarize acoustic and linguistic features in a way that is fundamentally independent of the input utterance's absolute length. This allows the model to be trained on varied-length speech segments while generalizing to arbitrarily long or short test utterances by maintaining a persistent, task-relevant context in its memory. The synergy produces a system that is both robust to extreme train-test length mismatches and capable of sophisticated long-range dependency tracking, such as holding consistent speaker state or dialogue history over extended conversations.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Lexical Decoding via Subword Composition
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from bioinformatics, specifically the hierarchical genome assembly process where short DNA reads are assembled into contigs using overlapping k-mers and reference scaffolds. The analogous problem in speech recognition is scaling from phoneme recognition to large-vocabulary word recognition. The adapted solution proposes a hierarchical lexical decoding system where the model's phoneme predictions are treated as short, reliable sequences. These are then composed into words using a statistical language model defined over a manageable inventory of subword units (like morphemes or syllables), which serves as a scaffold. This allows the system to recognize a vast vocabulary by constructing words from shared subword components, rather than requiring a monolithic phoneme-to-word mapping for every word in the lexicon, thereby improving scalability and generalization to unseen words.

ideas.title: Transfer hierarchical attention from documents
ideas.description: Use multi-level attention, first on regions then on objects within regions, for fine-grained description.
ideas.sourceTitle: Show, Attend and Tell: Neural Image Caption Generation with Visual Attention

ideas.title: Speech-Driven Video Synthesis via Large-Vocabulary Audio-Visual Alignment
ideas.description: This research fuses video generation from text with large-vocabulary speech recognition to create a model that synthesizes video directly from raw audio of continuous speech. The core innovation is learning a joint embedding space that aligns large-vocabulary spoken word sequences with visual dynamics and semantics, without an explicit text intermediary. This enables the generation of video content that is precisely synchronized with the narrative, emotional tone, and semantic content of free-form speech, such as a lecture or story. The resulting capability is a more natural and accessible content creation tool, where spoken description directly manifests as a dynamic visual scene.

ideas.title: End-to-End Differentiable Acoustic Representation Learning with Attention
ideas.description: This approach replaces fixed acoustic feature pipelines with a trainable neural front-end that projects raw waveform or low-level spectrograms into a latent feature space. This featureizer module is constructed with differentiable operations (e.g., convolutional filters with learnable parameters, gating mechanisms) and is jointly optimized with the subsequent attention-based model in a single end-to-end training loop. The attention mechanism can thus directly influence which low-level acoustic patterns are extracted and emphasized, creating a cohesive system where the representation is inherently task-adaptive. This eliminates the bottleneck of pre-defined, potentially suboptimal feature engineering, leading to more robust and higher-performing speech processing models.

ideas.title: Temporal Attention De-Sharpening via Counterfactual Context Injection
ideas.description: This research develops a training-time intervention that combats the sharpening of attention over time by systematically inverting the model's temporal focus. Instead of allowing attention to converge on a narrow set of salient past tokens, the method identifies and up-weights attention to temporal segments the baseline model deems least relevant for the current prediction. This forces the model to construct representations that integrate counterfactual or seemingly tangential temporal context, breaking the sharpness attractor. The resulting model learns to maintain a broader, more stable temporal receptive field, enhancing its ability to handle long-range dependencies and narrative coherence without catastrophic forgetting of earlier context.

ideas.title: Inverse: Vocabulary compression for deployment

ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Phrase Representations using RNN Encoder–Decoder for Statistical Machine Translation

ideas.title: Hierarchical Planning with Learned Alignment for Parallel Speech Synthesis
ideas.description: This system tackles the misalignment problem in parallel speech generation by decoupling high-level prosodic planning from detailed acoustic unit prediction. A lightweight planner first generates a non-autoregressive sequence of supra-segmental features, establishing a stable rhythmic and structural template. A subsequent parallel decoder, conditioned on this plan, predicts the acoustic sequence using a learned, soft alignment mechanism that is not strictly monotonic, allowing it to resolve local ambiguities by referencing the global plan. This results in robust, coherent, and fast speech generation that maintains natural prosody without autoregressive latency.

ideas.title: Apply to other multimodal tasks

ideas.title: Adversarially-Verified Meta-Learning for Robust Alignment Search
ideas.description: This approach trains an RL agent to explore attention-based alignment pathways within a meta-reinforcement learning framework. A key component is an adversarial verifier network that is trained to distinguish genuinely useful alignment explorations from reward-hacked artifacts, providing a stable, learned reward signal. The meta-learning outer loop optimizes the RL agent's policy to discover attention mechanisms that generalize robustly across a curated suite of alignment-relevant tasks and value landscapes. This creates a closed-loop system where the search process itself is aligned, preventing corruption of the alignment search space.

ideas.title: Scale-Invariant Architectures for Variable-Length Speech Sequences
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that training and test utterances must have similar lengths for effective speech processing. Drawing an analogy from computer vision, where scale-invariant models like CNNs with spatial pyramid pooling handle variable image sizes, we propose adapting this to the temporal dimension. The analogous solution involves extracting features in a way that is independent of input scale. The adapted research idea is to design a neural architecture for speech that uses a hierarchical temporal pooling mechanism (e.g., Temporal Pyramid Pooling) after initial feature extraction layers. This mechanism aggregates features across multiple temporal resolutions to produce a fixed-length representation, allowing the model to process training and test utterances of arbitrary and differing lengths without modification, thereby inverting the need for length similarity.

ideas.title: Hybrid Content-Position Memory for Robust Long-Form Speech Location Tracking
ideas.description: This system overcomes the addressing ambiguity of NTMs for speech by implementing a dual-path memory architecture with disentangled addressing. One path generates stable, temporally-aware positional keys via a learned convolutional timing signal, while the other performs standard content-based addressing. A trainable controller learns to attend to the positional memory for tracking speaker turns or dialogue state and to the content memory for semantic co-reference, preventing confusion when the same words recur. This enables robust maintenance of location and discourse structure over sequences far exceeding the limits of internal RNN state.

ideas.title: Compositional Length Generalization via Dynamic Complexity Curriculum and Efficient Routing
ideas.description: This research develops a training and architecture framework to improve robustness on long inputs by explicitly teaching the model to handle compositional complexity, not just token length. The system uses a generated curriculum that progressively increases sequence difficulty through operations like nesting, topic shifts, and injected long-range dependencies, simulating realistic long-document structures. A dynamic routing mechanism, such as a mixture of experts with learned capacity allocation, allows the model to focus computation on critical segments. The approach is validated by testing on held-out complexity operations and real-world long-context tasks, ensuring gains do not come at the cost of short-context performance.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Horizon Tracking to Sequence Attention
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysical seismic interpretation to the problem of informing attention mechanisms with convolutional features. In seismic analysis, convolutional filters are used to extract local texture and coherence features to track continuous horizons (geological layers) across noisy subsurface images. The adapted research idea treats an input sequence (e.g., text) as a 1D signal where certain semantic or syntactic features form continuous 'horizons'. A bank of learnable convolutional filters is applied to the sequence embeddings to generate multi-scale feature maps that highlight these locally consistent structural patterns. These convolutional feature maps are then used to bias or gate the attention distribution, compelling the model to focus its attention on coherent, contiguous segments of the sequence, thereby improving the model's ability to capture and follow meaningful, structured patterns within the data, analogous to spatial features guiding attention in image captioning.

ideas.title: Parallel Wavefront Decoding for Single-Pass Speech Generation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the autoregressive assumption in sequence generation by drawing an analogy from parallel solution methods in Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, wavefront or red-black Gauss-Seidel methods solve partial differential equations by updating non-adjacent grid points in parallel, allowing information to propagate bidirectionally and accelerate convergence. Adapted to speech generation, the target spectrogram is treated as a grid to be solved. A neural model is trained to perform parallel updates on a strategically selected subset of future time frames (a 'wavefront') based on a dynamically maintained context of all previously estimated frames, both past and future. This enables a single decoding pass where the overall structure emerges through iterative refinements of non-sequential frame blocks, blending non-autoregressive efficiency with bidirectional contextual reasoning for improved acoustic coherence and latency reduction.

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Attention for Audio-Visual Feature Refinement and Grounding
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel model that integrates a traditional acoustic feature extraction pipeline (e.g., MFCC, spectral contrast, pitch) directly into a cross-modal attention framework alongside visual features for video description. Instead of simply concatenating features, the model uses cross-attention layers to allow the visual stream to query and attend to specific acoustic feature dimensions, and the audio stream to attend to visual regions, creating a context-aware fusion. This synergy enables the generation of descriptions that not only mention visible objects and actions but also accurately attribute non-visual events (e.g., 'a door creaks,' 'enthusiastic applause') to their source in the video, achieving fine-grained audio-visual grounding that neither modality-pure attention nor simple early fusion allows.

ideas.title: Adaptive Noise Injection for Attention Stabilization
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from signal processing, where 'dithering' is used to prevent systems from narrowly locking onto a single dominant signal. The analogous problem is the 'capture effect' in receivers, solved by adding controlled noise to maintain sensitivity to a broader signal spectrum. Adapted to the research context of preventing overly narrow attention in transformers, the proposed solution is to develop an adaptive noise injection mechanism for attention stabilization. The method would inject structured, low-magnitude noise into the attention computation (e.g., to logits or projections), where the noise characteristics are dynamically adjusted based on the current entropy or concentration of the attention distribution. This approach provides a continuous, gradient-friendly alternative to sharpening transitions, encouraging the model to maintain a broader receptive field during training and inference, potentially improving robustness and multi-faceted reasoning.

ideas.title: Dynamically Regularized Attention via Entropic Constraints for Controlled Sharpness
ideas.description: This research first defines attention sharpness using the entropy of the attention distribution across a sequence, providing a rigorous, measurable foundation. A controller module is then trained alongside the main model to dynamically apply a regularization loss, ensuring the attention entropy remains within a learned, task-optimal band rather than being minimized or maximized arbitrarily. This approach moves beyond fixed penalties or transitions, enabling the model to adaptively balance focused and broad attention as needed for different inputs and prediction contexts. The system is evaluated by comparing the controlled entropy's correlation with performance on tasks known to suffer from both over- and under-attention.

ideas.title: Apply Topographic Map Principles to Sequence Attention
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience's topographic maps in sensory cortices, which enforce spatially organized connectivity based on input properties, this idea adapts that principle to sequence modeling. The analogous solution uses constrained local connectivity to preserve structural relationships. The adapted research idea is to design a location-aware attention mechanism where attention bias is not merely a fixed function of offset but is parameterized as a learnable topographic map over sequence positions. This allows the model to dynamically learn the optimal 'neighborhood' structure for attention—such as capturing local phrase dependencies in machine translation or the continuity of strokes in handwriting synthesis—integrating positional awareness directly into the attention weight calculation in a flexible, task-adaptive manner.

ideas.title: Transfer graph neural networks for feature relationships

ideas.title: Dynamic Encoder Refinement via Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that encoder outputs must remain static during attention by drawing an analogy from control systems engineering. In adaptive control systems, feedback loops continuously update internal models based on output errors. Similarly, we propose implementing feedback mechanisms from the decoder's attention patterns back to the encoder's hidden states. The encoder would initially produce representations, but these would be dynamically refined during the attention process based on what the decoder attends to, creating an iterative encoding process that adapts to the decoding task's specific needs rather than remaining fixed after the initial forward pass.

ideas.title: Interactive Disambiguation for Intent-Aware Video Generation from Text
ideas.description: This research proposes a system for video generation from text that explicitly tackles the inherent ambiguity of language by incorporating user-in-the-loop refinement. The model first generates or retrieves a small set of diverse video candidates that all plausibly match the input description. Through a concise interactive interface, the user provides feedback on these candidates, selecting preferences or correcting aspects like style, composition, or action specifics. This feedback is used to update a latent intent representation, guiding a final, personalized generation or retrieval step that produces a video aligned with the user's unique conceptualization.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Video Understanding via Hierarchical Task Decomposition
ideas.description: This research adapts the hierarchical and parallel processing solution from computational models of the primate visual system (e.g., HMAX) to the problem of joint video description and action recognition. The analogous field is computational neuroscience, where visual perception is solved through a hierarchy of brain areas that progressively extract more complex features, with specialized parallel streams for object identification and spatial/motion processing. The adapted idea is to train a single video understanding model with a biologically-inspired, multi-stream architecture. One specialized stream (analogous to the ventral pathway) will be optimized for decomposing scenes into objects, attributes, and their relationships to generate detailed video descriptions. A parallel, interacting stream (analogous to the dorsal pathway) will be optimized for integrating motion and spatial context to perform high-level action classification. The model will share low-level spatiotemporal feature extractors, then allow the pathways to diverge and specialize for their respective tasks, with lateral connections enabling the descriptive stream to inform action context and the recognition stream to guide descriptive relevance, thereby achieving efficient and synergistic dual-task learning.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Video Summarization via Multi-Scale Narrative Chunking
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical segmentation and historical reconstruction methods from geological stratigraphy to the problem of long-form video paragraph generation. In stratigraphy, geologists analyze rock layers (strata) at multiple temporal scales (e.g., eras, periods, epochs) to reconstruct a coherent historical narrative. Similarly, the proposed model would first perform multi-scale temporal chunking of a long video to identify hierarchical narrative units (e.g., acts, sequences, scenes). A hierarchical encoder would process these chunks to build a multi-scale representation, and a decoder would then generate a multi-sentence paragraph by first producing a high-level summary (the 'era' overview) and then recursively elaborating on the finer-scale events within that structure, ensuring the final paragraph is both comprehensive and coherent across temporal scales.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune System-Inspired RL for Attention-Based Alignment Search
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational immunology—the clonal selection and affinity maturation process of the adaptive immune system—to the problem of using reinforcement learning to train an attention mechanism for alignment search. In the analogous biological solution, a population of immune cells with random receptor variations is exposed to an antigen; high-affinity cells are cloned and undergo targeted hypermutation to refine the match, efficiently searching a vast combinatorial space. Adapted to this research context, the RL agent's policy network acts as a population of 'attention cells' that propose attention patterns (receptors) for exploring a model's latent alignment paths. Patterns yielding higher alignment reward (affinity) are selected, cloned, and their parameters are perturbed via a reward-guided hypermutation operator, creating a meta-learning loop that directs the attention mechanism's exploration capacity toward the most promising regions of the alignment search space, thereby accelerating and stabilizing the discovery of effective alignment trajectories.

ideas.description: This research first establishes a diagnostic benchmark based on a taxonomy of core temporal salience patterns found in real-world sequential data, such as key events, state changes, and periodic signals. It then uses this benchmark to perform a failure-mode audit of existing temporal attention mechanisms, identifying systematic biases and shortcomings. Finally, the work introduces new attention modules whose architectural priors are explicitly derived from and validated against the required temporal reasoning patterns, ensuring robust segment selection grounded in task semantics rather than benchmark overfitting.

ideas.title: Contextual Navigation via Cognitive Mapping for Frame Location
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that previous alignment is sufficient for location awareness by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience. In spatial navigation, biological systems solve the analogous problem of self-localization not through dead reckoning from a past point alone, but by building a cognitive map using rich contextual landmarks. The adapted solution is to move beyond simple temporal propagation for frame location features. For a video or image sequence, we would design a model that identifies and leverages frame-specific 'landmark' features—distinct visual or semantic elements that serve as anchors. A dedicated module, inspired by hippocampal place cells and context coding, would integrate these landmarks with the temporal stream to build a persistent, updatable spatial-contextual representation. This allows each frame's location feature to be informed by the broader environmental context of the sequence, enabling more robust, drift-resistant location awareness independent of strict frame-to-frame alignment.

ideas.title: Inductive Spatiotemporal Graph Networks for Physics-Aware Interaction Modeling in Video
ideas.description: This approach models object interactions in video with a graph neural network that dynamically infers relation types and strengths from multi-modal object features, rather than relying on pre-defined social network structures. It employs edge-conditioned convolutions where the kernel is generated by a learned function of spatiotemporal context, appearance, and motion features between nodes. An auxiliary learning objective enforces physical plausibility constraints, such as conservation of motion momentum in collisions. This results in a model that learns the underlying principles of interaction from data, making it robust to diverse scenes and interaction types unseen in training.

ideas.title: Temporal Ecological Network Transfer for Video Relation Modeling
ideas.description: This idea proposes transferring solution concepts from temporal ecological network analysis to the problem of modeling object interactions across time in videos. In ecology, researchers analyze how networks of species interactions (e.g., food webs, mutualistic networks) evolve over time, using methods to infer latent interaction rules from observed structural changes. We adapt this by treating objects in a video as analogous to species in an ecosystem, and their spatio-temporal relations (like pursuit, avoidance, or coordinated motion) as dynamic ecological links. A Graph Neural Network (GNN) architecture will be pre-trained or regularized using principles derived from ecological network models—such as stability constraints, interaction strength decay, or niche structure—and then fine-tuned on video data. This transfer leverages the robust, domain-general patterns of interaction dynamics found in ecological systems to improve relation modeling in videos, particularly when annotated video interaction data is scarce.

ideas.description: This system constructs a feature pyramid where frame-level location features are computed at multiple scales. A gating mechanism, conditioned on a learned estimate of local spatial uncertainty, controls the flow of information from coarser to finer scales. This ensures that broader contextual information is only incorporated when the local signal is ambiguous, preventing the model from being misled by irrelevant long-range correlations. The result is a location-aware representation that is both contextually informed and robust to spurious dependencies.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Partitioning for Hierarchical Temporal Attention
ideas.description: This idea investigates alternative architectures for the temporal attention module by drawing an analogy from ecological niche partitioning. In community ecology, species coexist by specializing in different resource niches. Adapted to temporal attention, this suggests designing a mechanism where different attention heads are specialized to attend to distinct types of temporal patterns or segments (e.g., rapid transitions, steady states, or periodic cycles), much like species specialize in different resources. By incorporating constraints or learning objectives that promote such niche specialization among heads, the module can achieve more efficient and comprehensive segment selection, reducing head redundancy and potentially improving model performance on complex temporal sequences.
